SAP Portfolio Briefing | May 2026 · FY2026 |
Consumed $14.6M Budget $16.5M ACV $4.1M
Abbott Laboratories
Life sciences and healthcare
YTD Budget: $1.8M
YTD Consumed: $1.5M
YTD ACV: $436K
Full-Year ACV: $436K
Full-Year Budget: $4.3M
EA Insights
Abbott's enterprise SAP footprint shows a consistent 'pipes contracted, processes dormant' pattern across every L1 domain: Integration Suite runs at 107-112% while SAP Build sits at 41% of plan; HANA Cloud is 35-40% under-utilized while SAC BI is throttled; Ariba Sourcing executes but Buying & Invoicing and Supplier Risk lag at 56% and 21%; C4C plateaus at ~60% while SIM pays out $52K/month; IBP burns ratably with no evidence of multi-module activation. Architecturally, Abbott has bought an integrated suite but is operating it as a federation of point tools — which means the cross-domain value chains (lead-to-cash, source-to-pay, plan-to-deliver, record-to-report) that justify SAP's premium over best-of-breed are not being realized, putting an estimated $400K+ of FY2026 ACV at structural renewal risk across domains.
The common dependency under every domain gap is the same: integration flows from Integration Suite are not landing in HANA Cloud persistence, are not feeding SAC for planning and BI, are not powering C4C-to-SIM crediting, and are not connecting Ariba Sourcing outcomes into Buying and Supplier Risk. Integration Suite is the only platform component running above plan, but its output is being consumed almost nowhere downstream — meaning Abbott's foundational middleware is succeeding technically while failing architecturally, because the business-process closures (closed-loop planning, closed-loop incentive, closed-loop procurement, closed-loop S&OP) it was bought to enable remain open.
Holistic contract posture shows Abbott has committed materially across five L1 areas (Supply Chain ~$1.48M annual / $618K YTD as the anchor, Finance & Spend with ~$337K SAC Planning + Ariba stack, BAIP Data & AI ~$107K at risk, BAIP Tech Foundation with the Build $3.5K gap, and CX with $13.8K/month C4C against $52K/month SIM) — but the value-realization gradient is inverted: the largest dollar commitments (IBP, SAC Planning, HANA Cloud) are the most architecturally dormant, while the smallest commitments (Integration Suite, SIM, Preferred Success Ariba) are saturated. Abbott is over-consuming the enablers and under-consuming the outcomes, which is the precise pattern that produces flat renewals and blocks expansion at FY2027.
Convene a single Enterprise Architecture Review with Abbott in Q2 FY2026 that maps the closed-loop business processes (source-to-pay, lead-to-cash-to-incentive, integrated business planning, plan-to-analyze) end-to-end across all L1 domains and identifies the 5-7 specific integration flows from Integration Suite that must terminate in HANA Cloud, SAC, SIM, Ariba Buying, and IBP to close each loop — replacing the current product-by-product true-up conversations with one architectural roadmap tied to documented business outcomes ahead of FY2027 renewal.
Reposition the FY2026 commercial conversation as a single enterprise renewal architecture rather than 12+ product true-ups: bundle the SAC BI expansion, HANA Cloud workload migration, Build activation, C4C capability activation, Ariba Buying/Supplier Risk lift, and IBP module activation under one executive sponsor at Abbott, using the over-consumed assets (Integration Suite, SIM, Preferred Success Ariba) as proof points that capacity expansion drives outcomes — protecting the ~$400K of at-risk ACV across domains in one motion.
Stand up an enterprise-level adoption scorecard within 60 days that measures cross-domain process completion (S&OP cycles closed, sourced suppliers transacting in Buying, CRM-sourced deals crediting in SIM, SAC dashboards persisted on HANA Cloud, Build apps deployed on Integration Suite APIs) rather than per-product dollar attainment — giving Abbott's CIO and SAP joint visibility into architectural value realization and creating the evidence base for FY2027 expansion rather than flat renewal.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
74.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$89K
YTD Consumed$66K
YTD ACV: $23K
Full-Year ACV: $23K · Full-Year Budget: $219K
Solution Insights
Abbott's BAIP Data & AI stack is architecturally lopsided: the analytics consumption layer (SAC BI) is throttled at its ACV ceiling with $933 of Q1 latent demand spilling over, while the underlying data foundation (HANA Cloud) sits 35-40% under-utilized at ~$8.8K/month against a $13.8K budget. This is a classic 'consumption-tier saturated, foundation-tier dormant' pattern — Abbott is extracting visualization value from SAC but has not landed the mission-critical workloads (analytics marts, Datasphere integrations, app persistence) that HANA Cloud is architected to anchor, leaving $107K of FY2026 ACV at risk of erosion.
The data integration spine (Data Intelligence Cloud) is the only product running to plan at 99.1% YTD attainment, but with 58% of annual ACV ($17,696) unscheduled for Jun-Dec, there is no orchestration roadmap visible in the back half. Architecturally this matters because DIC is the connective tissue that should be feeding HANA Cloud workloads and SAC BI datasets — without a loaded Jun-Dec pipeline, the under-utilization of HANA Cloud is unlikely to self-correct, since net-new integration use cases are what would drive persistence and analytics demand downstream.
Run a single combined workshop within 30 days that maps Abbott's SAC BI overage use cases backward to HANA Cloud persistence and DIC integration flows — use the $933 Q1 SAC overage as proof of latent analytics demand to justify migrating 2-3 workloads onto HANA Cloud, targeting a step-up from $8.8K to $13.8K+/month and closing the structural foundation gap before Q3.
Open a coordinated commercial conversation with Abbott covering all three products simultaneously: expand SAC BI ACV to cover the $13,860 Jun-Dec budget gap and prevent throttling, load DIC ACV across Jun-Dec to give the $17,696 remaining value a consumption ceiling to pace against, and commit HANA Cloud to a monthly attainment plan above 90% — treating these as one architectural renewal rather than three isolated true-ups.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
106.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$129K
YTD Consumed$137K
YTD ACV: $27K
Full-Year ACV: $27K · Full-Year Budget: $286K
Solution Insights
The Technology Foundation layer at Abbott is operating in an architecturally lopsided state: Integration Suite is functioning as the load-bearing backbone — sustaining 107-112% budget consumption every month and embedded in active production interfaces — while SAP Build, the low-code extensibility layer meant to capitalize on those integrations, is disengaging. Architecturally, Integration Suite exposes APIs and orchestrates data flows precisely so that Build can compose custom apps and workflows on top; Abbott is investing heavily in the pipes but not in the experiences and automations that monetize them, leaving extension and citizen-development capability dormant despite $3,553 in contracted ACV.
SAP Build's flat $112-$120/month band — a disengagement curve trending to ~41% of FY26 plan — indicates the platform's core capabilities (business app creation, process automation, workflow extension of SAP core) were used for an initial proof point and never scaled to new use cases or teams. This is a classic 'one-app-and-stop' adoption pattern: the low-code factory exists but has no pipeline of business-sponsored workloads feeding it, meaning Abbott is paying for an extensibility platform while continuing to absorb customization demand elsewhere (likely as custom code or shadow IT on top of the very integrations Integration Suite is successfully delivering).
Convene a joint Integration Suite + Build architecture review with Abbott in the next 30 days to map the ~$28K/month of active integration flows to candidate Build use cases — every high-value interface in production is a latent trigger for a workflow, approval app, or process automation, and surfacing 2-3 of these converts dormant Build ACV into business outcomes while reinforcing the Integration Suite expansion narrative.
Initiate an Integration Suite true-up now to lift the ACV ceiling ahead of the projected ~$44K Q4 shortfall, and package it with a Build activation commitment of $315+/month starting June under a named Abbott executive sponsor — bundling the true-up with a Build re-engagement plan protects the renewal of both products and prevents the $2,000+ Build ACV gap from hardening into a contraction at renewal.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
59.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$332K
YTD Consumed$198K
YTD ACV: $82K
Full-Year ACV: $82K · Full-Year Budget: $799K
Solution Insights
Abbott's Customer Experience architecture is split-brained: the front-office engagement layer (Cloud for Customer) is underutilized while the back-office monetization layer (SAP Incentive Management) runs at full throttle. SIM is processing ~$52K/month in commission calculations against a sales motion that C4C shows is only being executed by a narrow user base at ~60% of planned consumption — meaning Abbott is paying out incentives on pipeline and deals that are being managed largely outside the contracted CRM system of record, undermining the auditability and selling-behavior feedback loop SIM is architecturally designed to deliver.
The C4C plateau ($7.2K–$8.6K for five months against a $13.8K budget) is not a consumption problem, it is a capability-activation problem — opportunity management, activity capture, and mobile CRM appear dormant, leaving Cloud for Customer functioning as a contact database rather than the sales execution platform that should be feeding clean deal and quota data downstream into SAP Incentive Management. Without that integration live, SIM's 'improve selling behavior' and 'real-time performance insight' capabilities cannot be realized regardless of how cleanly it calculates payouts.
Run a joint C4C–SIM architecture workshop with Abbott's sales operations and comp admin leads to map which opportunity, quota, and crediting data flows from Cloud for Customer into Incentive Management today; commit to activating at least one additional C4C capability (opportunity or activity management) for one new business unit in the next 60 days so SIM is calculating against CRM-sourced deals, not spreadsheets — recovering ~$19K of the C4C budget gap by year end.
Open a joint ticket with Abbott's SIM admin and SAP support to root-cause the May -$51,754 entry (commission reversal, period reopen, or billing correction) and document it formally before the June QBR so the -98.1% May attainment is not misread as churn; lock the Jan–Apr ~$52,500/month run rate as the baseline and confirm June posts back in range to isolate the event.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
77.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$670K
YTD Consumed$516K
YTD ACV: $181K
Full-Year ACV: $181K · Full-Year Budget: $1.6M
Solution Insights
Abbott's Finance & Spend Management architecture shows a procurement stack that is contractually complete but operationally narrow — Ariba Sourcing (~87% attainment), Ariba Buying & Invoicing (~56% attainment, capped in a $15–21K band), and Ariba Supplier Risk (21% attainment, flat at ~$714/month) are all deployed but functioning as isolated point tools rather than an integrated source-to-pay process. Sourcing events are running, but their outcomes are not flowing into broader buying volume or supplier risk monitoring, meaning awarded suppliers are not being onboarded, transacted, or continuously risk-assessed at scale. The $120K YTD gap in Buying & Invoicing and the projected 78% shortfall in Supplier Risk are symptoms of the same architectural problem: Abbott is governing a narrow slice of spend end-to-end while the majority leaks to off-platform channels.
The planning and procurement value chains are both architecturally under-leveraged in opposite ways — SAC Planning (LPR001) is stalled at 53% utilization ($238K consumed vs $337K YTD budget) because it is confined to a single planning domain rather than unifying financial and operational planning as designed, while Preferred Success Ariba is saturated at 100%+ ACV ($138K consumed Jan–May), signaling Abbott is rationing the very advisory capacity that could drive the procurement adoption expansion needed elsewhere. The LoB has bought the capability to plan across workforce, capex, and sales and to accelerate Ariba adoption through Preferred Success, but is using neither at architectural intent — one is under-scoped, the other is ceiling-bound.
Convene a Q2 source-to-pay architecture review with Abbott's procurement leadership that treats Sourcing, Buying & Invoicing, and Supplier Risk as one connected process rather than three contracts: identify 2–3 high-spend categories currently sourced offline, migrate them through Ariba Sourcing, route awarded suppliers into Buying & Invoicing catalogs, and auto-enroll them into Supplier Risk monitoring — this single integrated motion addresses the $120K Buying gap, the $6.9K Sourcing gap, and the $13K Supplier Risk gap simultaneously by lifting transaction volume across the whole stack.
Reallocate the saturated Preferred Success Ariba engagement capacity toward a 60-day SAC Planning use-case expansion (headcount and OpEx planning) and a parallel Ariba adoption sprint, and open a renewal-cycle expansion conversation for Preferred Success now — this converts Abbott's one over-consumed asset into the delivery engine that closes the ~$215K projected SAC shortfall and the ~$183K projected Ariba Buying shortfall before FY26 close.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
100.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$618K
YTD Consumed$618K
YTD ACV: $124K
Full-Year ACV: $124K · Full-Year Budget: $1.5M
Solution Insights
Abbott's Supply Chain Management architecture in SAP is currently single-pillar: IBP is the only contracted planning capability, which means demand sensing, S&OP, inventory optimization, and supply response are all expected to flow through one platform. The flat $123,616/month consumption pattern — identical to the dollar across five months — is characteristic of a subscription draw, not a usage curve, so we cannot yet confirm whether Abbott is actually exercising the full IBP capability stack (demand, supply, S&OP, response) or just one module. Architecturally, IBP is designed as a unified collaborative planning process; if only forecasting is live while inventory optimization and response planning remain dormant, Abbott is paying for an integrated platform but running it as a point solution.
With $618,080 consumed YTD against a $1,483,404 annual contract and a clean glide path to 100% attainment, the financial signal is healthy but architecturally uninformative — ratable burn tells us nothing about planner engagement, planning cycle frequency, or scenario depth. For a planning platform whose value is realized in decisions made (S&OP cycles closed, demand plans published, supply scenarios modeled), dollar attainment is a lagging and misleading KPI. Abbott is at risk of arriving at FY2027 renewal with full consumption but no documented business outcomes, which weakens both expansion potential and renewal defensibility.
Convene a Q2 IBP architecture review with Abbott's supply chain planning leadership to map which IBP modules (demand, inventory, S&OP, response) are actively in production versus dormant, and prioritize activation of at least two underutilized modules — demand sensing and S&OP scenario planning are the highest-leverage candidates — to convert the flat subscription burn into measurable planning capability before H2 FY2026.
Stand up an IBP adoption scorecard that tracks planner logins, planning runs executed, forecast accuracy, and scenarios modeled monthly, and pair it with documented business outcomes (cycle time, forecast bias, inventory turns) to build the evidence base for an FY2027 expansion conversation rather than a flat renewal.
Border States Industries, Inc.
Wholesale distribution
YTD Budget: $454K
YTD Consumed: $410K
YTD ACV: $82K
Full-Year ACV: $82K
Full-Year Budget: $1.1M
EA Insights
Border States has built a federated SAP estate where each L1 domain operates as a vertical silo with no horizontal integration fabric activated — Employee Central is a system of record but doesn't feed Compensation, Commerce Cloud runs without Service/Marketing/CDP, Concur's Expense layer has decoupled from ExpenseIt/Pay/Audit, and BDC powers infrastructure but no Datasphere/SAC/Joule consumption surfaces. The architectural fact is that the customer has bought systems of record across every domain but has not invested in the cross-domain orchestration layer, which blocks the very outcomes (pay-for-performance, unified commerce, closed-loop spend, data-driven decisions) that justify a multi-LoB SAP footprint.
The Technology Foundation is the only LoB running hot (Joule Studio 209%, Integration Suite 119%) while every consuming domain around it is cold or flat — this inversion is architecturally telling: BTP is being burned by a small set of AI/integration use cases that are NOT wired into HCM, CX, Finance, or Data & AI workloads, meaning Border States is paying twice (once for BTP capacity, once for domain ACV) without the integration chain that would convert BTP spend into LoB outcomes. If Integration Suite were actually carrying the Performance→Compensation, Commerce→Service, or BDC→Datasphere flows, the consuming domains would not be sitting at 0–48% attainment.
At the enterprise contract level, Border States is operating a structurally unbalanced portfolio — Technology Foundation and Finance/Spend are over-consuming and need true-ups within 30–60 days, while Data & AI (62% unallocated on $215K), Customer Experience ($183K ACV gap on $224K), and HCM talent modules (Compensation at 0%, Career at 42%) leave well over $300K of contracted capacity dormant with only 7 months remaining. The enterprise is simultaneously under-capacity on the platform layer and over-capacity on the outcome layer, which is the signature of a customer that bought an integrated vision but is executing as disconnected point solutions, putting FY2026 renewal narratives at risk across three LoBs at once.
Convene an Enterprise Architecture Review in June with Border States' CIO, CHRO, CFO, and Head of Digital Commerce in one room to map the four broken integration chains (Performance→Compensation→EC Payroll; BDC→Datasphere/SAC; Commerce→Service/Marketing; Concur Expense→Pay/Audit) onto a single Integration Suite-anchored roadmap, converting the existing BTP overconsumption into the connective tissue that activates dormant ACV in Data & AI, CX, and HCM talent.
Run a consolidated FY2026 contract rebalancing motion within 45 days that pairs the Technology Foundation uplift (Joule + Integration Suite) and Concur true-up (~$17K) with explicit activation commitments on the under-consumed LoBs — SF Compensation for the merit cycle, one net-new Commerce Cloud workload, and one Data & AI service on BDC — so commercial expansion on hot LoBs is contractually tied to outcome activation on cold LoBs rather than negotiated as five independent conversations.
Launch a 90-day Clean Core and Integration Coherence workshop anchored on BTP to inventory where Joule agents and iflows are actually landing today vs. where the architectural design intended them to land, then publish a single enterprise integration map that governs BTP as one platform of record across all five L1 domains — this protects the next S/4HANA upgrade path and gives Border States a defensible renewal narrative that the SAP estate is one architecture, not five line items.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
93.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$87K
YTD Consumed$81K
YTD ACV: $0
Full-Year ACV: $0 · Full-Year Budget: $216K
Solution Insights
The Data & AI foundation is operationally embedded but architecturally narrow — Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (BDC) is the only active product in this LoB, and while it is consuming steadily (93.2% YTD attainment, 5 of 5 months tracking near or above plan), it is functioning as a standalone runtime rather than as the substrate for a broader Data & AI portfolio. Without companion services (e.g., Datasphere, SAC, Joule, AI Foundation) drawing on this platform, the LoB is paying for capacity that is powering infrastructure consumption rather than business-facing data and AI outcomes.
The annual contract is structurally under-shaped for the LoB's current usage pattern — $81,444 consumed against a $215,855 annual commitment leaves 62% of budget unallocated with only 7 months remaining, requiring a ~18% lift in monthly run-rate ($16.3K → $19.2K) to land the contract. This gap is not a consumption problem but an architectural one: the EA was sized for a wider Data & AI footprint than what is currently deployed, signaling that planned workloads (likely AI/ML, data fabric, or analytics extensions) were never activated on the platform.
Convene a June workload expansion review with the Data & AI LoB owner to map the original EA business case against deployed services, identify the 1–2 dormant Data & AI capabilities (Datasphere, SAC, AI Foundation, or Joule extensions) that were intended to run on this platform, and activate at least one to lift monthly run-rate toward $19.2K and convert infrastructure spend into LoB outcomes.
Document the May 113.7% attainment drivers and codify them as the baseline workload profile, then build a renewal narrative that either right-sizes the EA to actual architectural scope or commits the customer to a defined activation roadmap for the remaining Data & AI stack before FY2026 close.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
139.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$42K
YTD Consumed$58K
YTD ACV: $0
Full-Year ACV: $0 · Full-Year Budget: $102K
Solution Insights
The BTP foundation underpinning Border States' extensibility and integration strategy is operating beyond its contracted envelope across two distinct capability layers — Joule Studio (AI/agent development on BTP) is running at 209% of plan and Integration Suite (the iPaaS backbone for S/4 and surrounding systems) is at 119% YTD. Architecturally this signals that the Technology Foundation LoB has moved past pilot into embedded production use: Joule-based AI extensions and Integration Suite flows are now load-bearing for business processes, not experimental. The risk is not adoption — it is that the commercial envelope was sized for a foundation, while the customer is operating it as a platform of record.
The two products are functionally coupled and the consumption pattern reflects that coupling — Joule Studio agents and extensions almost always invoke Integration Suite endpoints to reach SAP and non-SAP systems of record, so Joule's 2x burn is very likely a leading indicator of further Integration Suite pressure in H2. With Joule on pace to exhaust its $24,551 annual budget by June and Integration Suite trending to ~$113K against a $77K plan, the LoB is approaching a compound capacity event where a hard stop on either product would stall the AI and integration roadmap simultaneously, not independently.
Convene a single joint consumption and architecture review within 2 weeks covering both Joule Studio and Integration Suite together — map which Joule agents/extensions are driving Integration Suite calls, classify workloads as strategic production vs. unoptimized/non-prod, and produce one consolidated 7-month forecast so the BTP foundation is governed as one platform rather than two line items.
Initiate a CPEA true-forward / capacity uplift conversation now, before the Joule budget exhausts in June, and structure it as a Technology Foundation expansion (Joule + Integration Suite) tied to the AI and integration roadmap; pair the commercial uplift with governance guardrails — tagging, environment cleanup, quotas on non-production Joule workspaces, and iflow consolidation — so the expanded envelope funds strategic growth rather than absorbing drift.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
32.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$54K
YTD Consumed$17K
YTD ACV: $19K
Full-Year ACV: $19K · Full-Year Budget: $132K
Solution Insights
Customer Experience for Border States is architecturally single-threaded on SAP Commerce Cloud — the platform is the designated system of engagement for B2B catalog management, pricing, promotions, and unified storefront experiences, but the flat $3.4K/month consumption signature indicates only one storefront or business unit is actually transacting. The 'grow sales across every channel' and 'seamless unified commerce' capabilities are contractually licensed but functionally dormant, meaning Border States is paying for a multi-channel commerce backbone while operating it as a single-catalog web shop.
The Customer Experience LoB has no surrounding CX architecture activated — Commerce Cloud is running in isolation without adjacent capabilities like Service, Marketing, or Customer Data that would normally drive traffic, personalization, and repeat buyer behavior into the storefront. This explains the locked consumption band: with $17.3K YTD against a $54.1K YTD budget (32% attainment) and a projected $41.5K annual landing against a $224.7K ACV ceiling, the storefront has no demand-generation or post-sale engagement engine feeding it, so consumption cannot expand organically.
Run a Commerce Cloud workload expansion workshop in the next 30 days to activate a second revenue stream on the existing platform — a second B2B catalog, punchout integration for electrical distribution buyers, or a new customer segment — since the flat consumption pattern will not break without a net-new workload, and one comparable workload would roughly double monthly run-rate and lift attainment toward 65%.
Build a 7-month catch-up plan targeting ~$15K/month consumption anchored to Border States revenue milestones (peak ordering season, distributor onboarding waves) rather than generic feature rollout, so adoption is tied to measurable commerce events that close the $76K ACV gap before fiscal year end.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
116.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$57K
YTD Consumed$67K
YTD ACV: $16K
Full-Year ACV: $16K · Full-Year Budget: $137K
Solution Insights
Border States' Travel & Expense architecture is operationally embedded but structurally undersized — Intelligent Audit, ExpenseIt, and Reporting Services are all consuming at 100% of ACV ceiling month after month (124-137% attainment), which means the compliance, receipt capture, and analytics layers of the Concur stack are delivering intended value but have no headroom left for additional users, categories, or audit volume. The LoB has outgrown its contracted capacity across three interdependent products simultaneously, signaling the FY2026 contract was sized against a stale baseline.
The expense submission front-end has decoupled from the rest of the stack — Concur Expense consumption collapsed 92% in May while ExpenseIt (receipt capture), Expense Pay (reimbursement), and Intelligent Audit (compliance) continue running at or above ceiling. Architecturally this is incoherent: receipts are being scanned and audits are firing, but expense reports are not flowing through the system of record. This points to a workflow, approval, or policy disruption at the Expense layer that will starve the downstream Pay and Audit capabilities if not resolved within the current cycle.
Convene the AP/Finance owner this week to diagnose the Concur Expense May cliff against the still-healthy ExpenseIt and Expense Pay run rates — if receipts are being captured but reports are not submitting, the break is in approval workflow or policy configuration, not user adoption, and must be fixed before June to protect the integrity of the end-to-end T&E process.
Open a consolidated ACV true-up conversation covering ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, and Reporting Services in a single contract motion within 30 days — present the combined projected overage (~$17K across the three products) and reset ceilings together, since these capabilities scale as one workflow and piecemeal expansion will leave the LoB hitting hard limits sequentially through Q3.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
87.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$214K
YTD Consumed$187K
YTD ACV: $47K
Full-Year ACV: $47K · Full-Year Budget: $517K
Solution Insights
The HCM foundation is architecturally sound but the talent layer is fundamentally disconnected — Employee Central (100% May attainment) and HXM Operations (100%) are operationally embedded as the system of record, yet the talent products that depend on EC data (Career & Talent Development at 42%, Onboarding at 48%, Compensation at 0%) are running as isolated pilots or stalled deployments. Border States has built the HR data backbone but is not capitalizing on the integrated talent experience SuccessFactors is designed to deliver, leaving EC as an expensive HRIS rather than the foundation for skills-driven workforce decisions.
The pay-for-performance architecture is broken at the most critical integration point — SF Performance & Goals is running steady at 94.7% attainment producing performance data, but SF Compensation sits at 0% consumption, meaning there is no downstream system consuming that performance signal to drive merit, bonus, or equity decisions. Combined with Career & Talent Development stuck on autopilot at 42%, the talent-to-reward closed loop (Performance → Compensation → Career Development) is architecturally contracted but operationally severed, and Border States will miss the FY2026 merit cycle entirely if Compensation is not activated within 30 days.
Convene an urgent HCM architecture review with Border States HR leadership to activate SF Compensation against the FY2026 merit cycle as the anchor use case, explicitly wiring Performance & Goals outputs into Compensation worksheets and EC payroll execution — this single integration unlocks the pay-for-performance loop and protects the $550 at-risk budget while justifying the broader talent stack investment.
Launch a Q2 talent activation program that leverages the stable EC foundation to expand Career & Talent Development (Development Plans, Career Worksheets, Mentoring) and Onboarding (mandate as system of record for all new hires) to at least two additional employee populations — targeting Career Development to ~$3,500/month and Onboarding to ~$2,300/month by August to convert EC's system-of-record status into measurable talent outcomes before renewal.
Domino's Pizza LLC
Consumer products
YTD Budget: $866K
YTD Consumed: $803K
YTD ACV: $207K
Full-Year ACV: $207K
Full-Year Budget: $2.1M
EA Insights
Domino's has contracted an integrated SAP enterprise stack — BTP as the extensibility and integration backbone, HANA Cloud as the data foundation, IBP + Business Network as the demand-to-supply loop, Ariba S2C+P2P as the procurement spine, and Concur for T&E — but the architecture is splintering into two halves: the *transactional and integration plumbing* layers (Integration Suite at 99%, Ariba Buying at 99.96%, Concur stack at 108–123%) are healthy or over-running, while every *strategic, planning, and value-creation* layer above them (SAP Build 52.7%, HANA Cloud 75.2%, Ariba Sourcing/Contracts 66%, IBP and Business Network flat-lined) is disengaging. The big-picture consequence: Domino's is paying ~$1M+ in FY2026 contracts to run the business but is no longer using SAP to *improve* the business — pizza is moving, invoices are paying, APIs are flowing, but sourcing rigor, supply chain resilience, AI-driven insight, and net-new digital experiences are all stalled, which is exactly the capability set a QSR leader needs to defend margin against commodity inflation and franchisee pressure in FY2026.
The architectural pattern across three independent L1 domains points to one shared root cause — a missing 'data + extensibility' activation layer. HANA Cloud is stagnating because Datasphere/SAC aren't pulling on it; SAP Build is collapsing because no new apps are being authored on top of Integration Suite; IBP and Business Network are flat because supplier-side data isn't being wired into the planning brain. All three are symptoms of the same gap: Domino's has no active program turning contracted SAP capability into net-new data products, workflows, or supplier integrations. Without this activation layer, every other domain will continue to consume only its transactional floor, and Domino's BTP-anchored clean-core ambition (extensions on the platform, not in S/4) cannot materialize — meaning customizations will inevitably leak back into the core and create upgrade debt.
Domino's FY2026 contract position is structurally mis-shaped against actual demand, and the mis-shaping is bidirectional within the same enterprise: Concur T&E will breach ACV ceilings between August and October (service-disruption risk on a working capability), while BTP Build, Custom Domain, HANA Cloud, Ariba Sourcing/Contracts, IBP, and Business Network will collectively leave an estimated $200K+ unrealized by year-end (renewal-risk on dormant capabilities). The enterprise is simultaneously over-consuming what works and under-consuming what was bought to transform — a clear signal that FY2026 was contracted to an architectural ambition the customer has not operationalized, and FY2027 renewal conversations will be fragmented and defensive unless the portfolio is rebaselined as one coherent commercial decision.
Convene a single Enterprise Architecture Review with Domino's CIO, CDO, and LoB owners (Procurement, Supply Chain, Finance, Digital) within 45 days, framed around one question: 'Which 5 cross-domain business outcomes will Domino's commit to delivering on the SAP stack in H2 FY2026?' — for example, supplier-collaboration-to-IBP-to-HANA closed-loop resiliency, Sourcing-to-Contracts-to-Buying compliance restoration, and Build+Integration Suite+Custom Domain franchisee/loyalty app launches. This replaces four disconnected product conversations with one architectural commitment and creates pull-through demand across the dormant layers simultaneously.
Stand up a 'BTP Activation Squad' (joint SAP + Domino's, 90-day mandate) whose sole charter is to land 3–5 net-new workloads that consume the underutilized platform layer end-to-end: each workload must touch HANA Cloud (data), Datasphere/SAC (consumption), SAP Build (app/workflow), Integration Suite (already healthy), and Custom Domain (branded surface). Candidate workloads — franchisee operations portal, supplier onboarding with Business Network handoff, store-level demand sensing into IBP — are deliberately chosen to also pull the stalled Supply Chain and Data & AI domains forward, fixing four L1 gaps with one coordinated motion.
Initiate a consolidated FY2026 contract rebaselining exercise with Domino's CFO and procurement, presenting the entire SAP portfolio as one position: true-up Concur T&E (breach risk by Q3), reconcile Ariba Buying's $68K back-half headroom, and put BTP, HANA Cloud, IBP, and Business Network under-consumption on the table as capability-activation commitments rather than passive shelfware. The goal is to convert FY2027 from four fragmented renewal defenses into one enterprise renewal anchored to the activation roadmap from the EA Review, protecting ~$200K of at-risk ACV and creating a credible expansion path on the over-running domains.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
75.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$54K
YTD Consumed$41K
YTD ACV: $13K
Full-Year ACV: $13K · Full-Year Budget: $133K
Solution Insights
The Data & AI foundation for Domino's is architecturally narrow — SAP HANA Cloud is the sole active component, with no evidence of the surrounding Business Data Cloud fabric (SAP Datasphere for data product modeling, SAP Analytics Cloud for consumption, or agentic AI anchoring) being engaged. This means HANA Cloud is being used as a standalone database rather than as the compute backbone of a business data fabric, which explains why workloads are stagnating: without Datasphere-driven data products and SAC-driven analytical consumption pulling on it, there is no demand engine to scale HANA Cloud usage.
The consumption trajectory confirms the architectural under-utilization — HANA Cloud spend has declined from $8.8K (Jan) to $7.7K (May) against a rising $11K budget, tracking to leave ~39% (~$63K) of the $160.8K annual contract unrealized. At 75.2% YTD attainment and falling, this is not a ramp-up lag; it is a signal that no new analytical or AI workloads are being onboarded onto the platform, and the contracted capacity for mission-critical data and AI use cases is sitting idle.
Run a Business Data Cloud architecture workshop within 30 days to position HANA Cloud alongside Datasphere and SAC, and identify 2–3 concrete data product or AI use cases (e.g., store-level demand signals, delivery analytics, agentic AI on operational data) that will drive HANA Cloud consumption from $7.7K back to the $11K monthly budget line starting June.
Establish a monthly consumption and capability-activation review with Domino's data platform owner, tracking both budget attainment (target 90%+ by August) and the activation of adjacent BDC capabilities, so that workload onboarding is governed against the full architectural intent of the contract rather than HANA Cloud usage in isolation.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
81.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$109K
YTD Consumed$88K
YTD ACV: $30K
Full-Year ACV: $30K · Full-Year Budget: $261K
Solution Insights
Domino's BTP technology foundation is architecturally provisioned for an extensibility-and-integration play, but the value-creation layer is disengaging while the plumbing holds — SAP Integration Suite (the API/integration backbone) is the only product still tracking near plan at 99% YTD attainment, while SAP Build (the low-code app and workflow layer that should be consuming those APIs) has slid to 52.7% attainment and is trending toward only 40% of its $159,972 ACV. In practice, Domino's has built the pipes but stopped building the apps that ride on them, meaning the integration investment is delivering connectivity without the citizen-developer and workflow extensions it was contracted to enable.
The customer-facing and platform-runtime layers are showing classic shelfware signatures that undermine the foundation's go-to-market role — SAP Custom Domain service (which fronts BTP apps with Domino's branded endpoints for commerce, loyalty, and franchisee portals) is declining monthly toward ~32% of ACV, and Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement consumption has dropped 30% from its March peak, projecting ~$198 of unused contract. Together this signals that net-new BTP workloads and customer-facing app surfaces are not being launched — without new Build apps to deploy and brand, both the domain service and the CPEA runtime have nothing to grow into, putting the entire Technology Foundation at renewal risk despite a healthy integration layer.
Run a joint Build + Integration Suite use-case workshop with Domino's BTP architect within 30 days to identify 2–3 net-new low-code apps (e.g., franchisee operations workflow, supplier onboarding, loyalty extension) that explicitly consume existing Integration Suite flows and are published via Custom Domain endpoints — this re-links the dormant app layer to the active integration backbone and creates pull-through demand across Build, Custom Domain, and CPEA simultaneously.
Establish a monthly Technology Foundation consumption governance review with Domino's technical owner covering all four products together, with a Q2 target of restoring SAP Build to ~$9,400/month, CPEA to $170+/month, and activating 2–3 new branded domains; treating these as one architectural stack (rather than four separate line items) is the only way to reverse the widening ACV gaps before H2 renewal conversations begin.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
98.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$353K
YTD Consumed$347K
YTD ACV: $98K
Full-Year ACV: $98K · Full-Year Budget: $852K
Solution Insights
The Source-to-Contract spine is eroding in lockstep — Ariba Sourcing and Ariba Contracts have both declined identically for three consecutive months (86% → 82% → 66%), which is not coincidence but architectural cause-and-effect. Sourcing events feed negotiated terms into Contracts as the legal backbone; when category managers revert to offline RFx, contract authoring volume mechanically drops behind it. Meanwhile Ariba Buying and Invoicing remains on-plan YTD (99.96%), meaning Domino's is still transacting POs and invoices but increasingly without upstream sourcing rigor or contract governance — the P2P tail is executing against agreements that aren't being refreshed in the system of record, putting compliance, savings capture, and supplier accountability at risk. Combined ~$43K of projected unrealized value across Sourcing and Contracts signals the strategic procurement layer is being bypassed while the transactional layer carries the illusion of health.
The Travel & Expense stack is the inverse problem — architecturally it is firing on all cylinders and structurally under-contracted. Concur Expense (123% YTD), ExpenseIt (108%), Analytics (118%), and Preferred Success (114%) are all over-consuming in a mutually reinforcing pattern: ExpenseIt's mobile OCR is driving more receipts into Expense, which generates more spend data into Analytics, which is consuming advisory hours via Preferred Success to operationalize insights. This is exactly the integrated T&E value loop the architecture is designed to produce. However, every component will breach its ACV ceiling before Q4 (Expense ~August, ExpenseIt pre-Q3, Preferred Success ~October), meaning the LoB faces a service-disruption cliff precisely because adoption succeeded — the FY2026 contract was sized to a demand curve that no longer reflects reality.
Convene a single Source-to-Contract recovery review with Domino's procurement leadership covering Sourcing and Contracts jointly (not as separate product conversations). Identify 2–3 upcoming categories — packaging, ingredients, logistics — to route through Ariba Sourcing in the next 60 days with a mandate that awards flow into Ariba Contracts authoring, re-establishing the architectural handoff. Pair this with a category-manager enablement refresh on event templates and contract workflows to reverse the disengagement pattern before it normalizes across Q3.
Initiate a consolidated T&E contract true-up covering Concur Expense, ExpenseIt, Analytics, and Preferred Success in one motion rather than four separate expansions. Frame it to Domino's finance leadership as right-sizing a proven, integrated capability — bring the run-rate projections (Expense breach ~August, Preferred Success exhaustion ~October) and the cross-product reinforcement story as the business case. Simultaneously reconcile Ariba Buying's $68K remaining ACV against the $350K back-half budget so the P2P contract position is rebaselined alongside T&E, giving Domino's one coherent FY2026 Finance & Spend coverage decision.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
93.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$349K
YTD Consumed$326K
YTD ACV: $65K
Full-Year ACV: $65K · Full-Year Budget: $903K
Solution Insights
Domino's supply chain planning architecture is structurally incomplete — IBP is provisioned as the planning brain for demand sensing, S&OP, and supply response, while Business Network Supply Chain Collaboration is the connective tissue that should feed supplier forecasts, PO commitments, and ASN signals back into IBP. Both products show identical flat-line consumption fingerprints ($9,308 and $55,968 monthly with zero variance for five months), meaning neither the planning engine nor the collaboration network is scaling. The LoB has bought the integrated demand-to-supply loop but is operating it as two dormant subscriptions rather than a closed-loop process.
The Business Network is functioning as a billing entity rather than a trading partner ecosystem — its architectural value (forecast collaboration, PO collaboration, ASN visibility, procurement resiliency) compounds with supplier onboarding and transaction volume, yet the flat $55,968/month against a budget curve ramping to $74,426 by December reveals a $23K YTD gap widening to $75K+ by year-end. For a business like Domino's with thousands of ingredient, packaging, and equipment suppliers, a static network means supply chain resiliency capabilities are paid for but not protecting the business from disruption.
Launch a joint IBP–Business Network activation initiative that wires supplier forecast commits and PO collaboration flows from the Network directly into IBP's supply response and S&OP planning areas — this closes the architectural loop both products were designed to deliver and creates measurable transaction volume on the Network while expanding IBP scenario usage beyond the current flat baseline.
Convene an executive business review with Domino's supply chain leadership covering both products together, presenting a unified adoption scorecard (active planners, scenarios run, connected suppliers, PO/ASN transaction volume) tied to the combined $186K annual investment, and co-build a Q3–Q4 FY2026 roadmap to onboard top non-digitized suppliers and activate 1–2 net-new IBP use cases such as demand sensing or inventory optimization.
Eli Lilly & Company
Life sciences and healthcare
YTD Budget: $7.6M
YTD Consumed: $7.3M
YTD ACV: $1.8M
Full-Year ACV: $1.8M
Full-Year Budget: $18.8M
EA Insights
Eli Lilly has contracted for an AI-and-process-driven enterprise architecture (Joule Studio at 171% of plan, Signavio PM expanded 2.5x, SAC BI throttled at its ceiling, Ariba transactional engines pinned at 100%) but the integration and extensibility fabric that should connect them is dormant — Integration Suite is flatlined at ~$25K/month with $24.6K YTD unused, BDC Public has collapsed 79%, and BTP credits earmarked for Integration Suite workloads are down 54%. The enterprise is simultaneously buying the agent surface, the process intelligence surface, and the analytics surface while starving the governed API substrate underneath, which means every new AI agent, process model, or planning workload at Lilly is on a trajectory to either bypass governance via point-to-point integrations or stall waiting for trusted APIs — a clean-core architecture is being eroded in real time.
Across every L1 domain Lilly has fully consumed the Preferred Success/enablement wrapper (PS Ariba locked at $84.8K, PS Commerce at 100% ACV, PS Signavio at 113%, PS SFSF burning ratably) while the underlying platforms those services are meant to accelerate are materially under-adopted (HXM Operations at 38%, Commerce Cloud at 45%, Signavio PI at 19%, SAC Planning at 4.5%, DRC at 0.17%, Batch Release Hub at 0.08%, IBP modules dormant). The success motion is architecturally disconnected from the platform consumption it should be driving — Lilly is paying full price for guided adoption that is not landing as production workloads, which means the FY27 renewal conversation will be defending shelfware across six domains unless the success entitlements are re-pointed at named platform gaps now.
Lilly's pharma-critical GxP and regulatory architecture has the compliance-adjacent layers live (SF Validated Learning at 96%, SSO trending to overage, Ariba P2P at ceiling) but the regulated execution and reporting layers that close the GxP loop are dark — Batch Release Hub is at $0 with $32K/month ACV burning, Document & Reporting Compliance is at 0.17% across 90+ countries, IBP Supply/Inventory/Response modules are dormant, and SAC Planning has never industrialised. For a pharma manufacturer, this means the digital thread from demand signal → supply plan → batch release → statutory reporting is broken at three of four nodes, leaving Lilly exposed on the exact controls (electronic batch disposition, e-invoicing/VAT, integrated S&OP) that regulators and finance auditors scrutinise — and leaving roughly $300K+ of annualised ACV commercially undefended at renewal.
Convene a single enterprise architecture review in the next 30 days with Lilly's CIO, Integration CoE, and platform owners to formally rebalance the BTP envelope: redirect dormant Integration Suite credits and BDC Public capacity (~$255K+ projected unconsumed) onto a named 7-month pipeline of AI-consumable APIs that explicitly underpin the Joule Studio overrun, the Signavio process threads, the Ariba P2P scale, and the IBP-to-Make/Deliver integration — turning three separate overage/shortfall conversations into one governed clean-core integration roadmap before Q3 contract motions begin.
Launch an enterprise-wide 'Preferred Success re-pointing' program in June that inventories every fully-consumed success entitlement (Ariba, Commerce, Signavio, SFSF, BTP) and contractually ties each remaining hour to a named under-adopted platform workload — HXM Operations process areas, a second Commerce storefront, a Signavio PI→PM closed-loop thread, SAC Planning go-live, and DRC statutory scenario — so the ~$200K+ of success spend in H2 produces documented platform consumption lift rather than generic advisory, and the FY27 renewal is sized on outcomes not entitlements.
Escalate a GxP and regulated-execution activation workstream to Lilly's Quality, Manufacturing IT, FP&A, and Tax sponsors within 2 weeks covering Batch Release Hub, IBP Supply/Inventory/Response, SAC Planning, and Document & Reporting Compliance as a single pharma-critical thread — commit to one pilot go-live per capability before end of Q3 (one site batch release, one IBP module beyond Demand, one planning model, one statutory reporting scenario) to convert ~$300K of dormant ACV into a defensible digital-thread narrative ahead of renewal sizing.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
108.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$65K
YTD Consumed$70K
YTD ACV: $3K
Full-Year ACV: $3K · Full-Year Budget: $152K
Solution Insights
Eli Lilly's BTP footprint is architecturally bifurcated and the two halves are telling opposite stories: the Database Cloud instance of the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is running at a sustained 135.6% of plan ($32,246 consumed vs $23,777 budgeted YTD, projecting ~$77K against a $56K annual envelope), while the BDC Public instance has collapsed 79% from January's $7,468 to ~$1,460/month — leaving $36K of the $58K annual budget likely unconsumed. This means the data persistence and database services layer of BTP is anchored to real production workloads at Lilly, but the broader platform extensibility layer (integration, extensions, data services) that BTP is designed to deliver around the SAP landscape never industrialised past early-year experimentation — Lilly is buying a platform and using only the database tier of it.
The analytics capability is deployed and demonstrably in demand but is being capacity-constrained rather than capability-constrained: SAC BI ran 25% over plan in Q1 ($3,524 vs $2,819/month) and then flatlined to exactly $2,819 in April and May, a signature of a hard tenant or license ceiling, not softening demand. Architecturally Lilly has the visualization and BI surface of SAC live, but the unified analytics + planning value proposition (financial/operational planning, Joule-assisted analytics) cannot land while users are being throttled on the BI tier — and at the current $2,819 run rate annual consumption projects to ~$36K against a $31.6K ACV, turning a latent upsell into an overage conversation.
Re-baseline the Database Cloud BTP contract now and simultaneously launch a 7-month BDC Public activation roadmap: take the five-month 135% over-attainment evidence and projected $77K full-year landing to Lilly procurement to scope an expansion order before Q3, and in the same conversation sequence 2–3 concrete BDC Public use cases (integration flows, extensions, data services tied to the existing SAP landscape) targeting ~$5,200/month so the unified BTP story is one expansion, not one overrun and one shortfall.
Confirm within 30 days whether the SAC BI April/May flatline at exactly $2,819 is a capacity or licensing ceiling, and if so convert it into a tier expansion while Q1 over-pull evidence is fresh — pair the uplift with an architectural roadmap that extends Lilly from SAC BI into SAC Planning and Joule-enabled analytics, so the next contract conversation is about unified analytics + planning maturity rather than right-sizing a BI overage.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
66.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$295K
YTD Consumed$196K
YTD ACV: $30K
Full-Year ACV: $30K · Full-Year Budget: $708K
Solution Insights
The BTP foundation at Lilly is splitting into two divergent trajectories that undermine the integration architecture's intent. Joule Studio consumption on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is running 71% above plan ($42K YTD vs $24K budgeted), signaling aggressive AI agent build-out, while the BTP credits earmarked for SAP Integration Suite workloads have collapsed 54% from January to May ($27K consumed vs $131K YTD budget). Architecturally, Integration Suite is the secure API layer that makes Joule agents enterprise-grade — Lilly is scaling the agent surface faster than the governed integration substrate that should feed it, which sets up a path where AI agents either consume ungoverned point-to-point integrations or stall waiting for trusted APIs.
The standalone SAP Integration Suite ACV ($30K/month ceiling) tells the same story from the other side: consumption is flatlined in a tight $24.8K–$26K band for five straight months, leaving $24.6K YTD unused and tracking toward ~52% full-year attainment. This is not an enablement problem — Lilly has a working integration practice — it is a scope problem. The same set of iFlows is being run month after month with no expansion into new S/4 interfaces, B2B partners, or AI-ready APIs, meaning the Integration Suite is operating as a steady-state middleware utility rather than the agent-and-app integration fabric it is architected to be, precisely at the moment Joule Studio adoption demands more APIs.
Convene a joint Joule Studio + Integration Suite architecture review with Lilly's platform and integration CoE owners within 30 days to map the AI agent roadmap driving the Cloud Platform overrun to the specific APIs and iFlows those agents will require. Use the output to (a) commercially true-up the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement before Q3 to cover the projected $101K Joule run rate, and (b) commit 2–3 net-new AI-consumable integration scenarios into Integration Suite to lift monthly consumption from $25K toward the $28.5K budget line by August.
Launch a BTP consumption rebalancing plan that redirects the dormant Integration Suite credits inside the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (~$255K projected unconsumed) toward a named 7-month workload pipeline — targeting $20K+/month — anchored on extension and integration use cases that close the architectural gap behind Joule (e.g., R&D data APIs, supply chain B2B onboarding, S/4 event-driven flows). Tie each workstream to a business owner and go-live date so the conversation shifts from shelfware reporting to a governed adoption commitment.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
48.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$96K
YTD Consumed$47K
YTD ACV: $50K
Full-Year ACV: $50K · Full-Year Budget: $245K
Solution Insights
Eli Lilly's Business Transformation Management stack is architecturally coherent but operationally lopsided — Signavio Process Management (the modeling and governance backbone) and Process Intelligence (the data-driven discovery layer) are both deployed, yet Process Intelligence sat dormant Jan–Mar and only went live in April at 19% YTD attainment, while Process Management saw a 2.5x ACV expansion to $41,169 with consumption actually dropping to $1,902 in May. The LoB has contracted for a closed-loop transformation capability (discover → model → govern → re-measure) but is currently running each tool as an isolated pilot, meaning process insights generated in Signavio PI are not feeding modeled to-be processes in Signavio PM, and the expanded PM ceiling has no discovery pipeline behind it to justify the commitment.
Preferred Success — the enablement and adoption accelerator for the Signavio suite — is the only product hitting its ceiling (113%+ attainment, $25,229 YTD vs $23,864 budget), which architecturally signals that Lilly's CoE is hungry for guided adoption but the underlying platform entitlements (PI and PM) are not yet absorbing that guidance into production usage. The success motion is outpacing the platform consumption it is meant to drive, indicating Preferred Success hours are likely being spent on foundational enablement and use-case scoping rather than scaling live process transformation work.
Re-architect the engagement around a single end-to-end process thread (e.g., Order-to-Cash or GxP-regulated Procure-to-Pay) where Process Intelligence mines the as-is, Process Management documents and governs the to-be, and Preferred Success hours are explicitly tied to that thread — target $6K+/month PI and $4.5K+/month PM consumption through Q3 to close the combined ~$70K YTD gap and prove the closed-loop value before renewal.
Convert the Preferred Success overage into a formal expansion conversation paired with a Process Management ramp plan: use the 113% PS attainment as evidence of CoE demand, true-up the PS entitlement to remove throttling, and contractually tie the expanded PM ceiling ($41,169 ACV) to named modeler cohorts and process repositories so the May ACV expansion has identifiable consumers rather than leaving over half the contract value unused by December.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
65.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$185K
YTD Consumed$121K
YTD ACV: $62K
Full-Year ACV: $62K · Full-Year Budget: $462K
Solution Insights
The Customer Experience architecture at Eli Lilly is anchored on a single storefront pattern — SAP Commerce Cloud is the system of engagement for B2B/B2C digital commerce, yet consumption has flat-lined at $12,049 for two consecutive months against a $26,714 monthly budget, indicating only one workload (likely a single Lilly Direct storefront) is live while the multi-channel, catalog, and B2B2C capabilities the platform was contracted to deliver remain dormant. At 45% YTD attainment ($57,285 of $49,554 monthly ACV trajectory), the LoB owns enterprise commerce capacity but is operating it as a point solution.
The success layer is fully drawn but the platform it governs is underused — Preferred Success Commerce is consuming exactly $12,700/month (100% of its ACV ceiling, 113–115% budget attainment) while SAP Commerce Cloud sits at ~45% of plan. Architecturally this is inverted: the advisory, release-readiness, and adoption guidance entitlement is being fully consumed, yet that guidance is not translating into expanded Commerce Cloud workloads, meaning Eli Lilly is paying for and attending success engagements that are not landing as new storefronts, channels, or transaction volume.
Redirect the remaining $88,900 of Preferred Success Commerce capacity (Jun–Dec) explicitly toward Commerce Cloud expansion outcomes — pre-book the architecture reviews and adoption workshops against a named second storefront (Lilly Direct international, animal health, or B2B specialty) so the success entitlement produces measurable Commerce Cloud consumption lift rather than generic advisory hours.
Run a Commerce Cloud activation and use-case expansion workshop within 30 days to diagnose the April–May flat-line (configuration cap, traffic, or catalog scope) and commit to a 90-day sprint that onboards a second channel or business unit, targeting at least $20K monthly Commerce Cloud consumption by August to close the $37,505 ACV gap before renewal sizing conversations begin.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
99.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$5.8M
YTD Consumed$5.8M
YTD ACV: $1.3M
Full-Year ACV: $1.3M · Full-Year Budget: $14.4M
Solution Insights
Eli Lilly's Procurement architecture is firing on its transactional spine but starving its governance and enablement layers — Ariba Buying & Invoicing and Ariba Sourcing are both pinned at 100% ACV ceiling for 3-5 consecutive months ($3.33M and $1.97M YTD respectively), proving the P2P and strategic sourcing engines are mission-critical to Lilly's operations, yet Preferred Success Ariba has flatlined at $84.8K since April with zero incremental engagement. The LoB is executing high-volume procurement transactions without consuming the CSM-led architecture reviews, release readiness, and adoption guidance that would optimise them — meaning Lilly is scaling Ariba usage faster than it is scaling Ariba maturity, and the contract envelope across all three Procurement SKUs is structurally undersized versus demonstrated demand.
Lilly's Finance & Compliance architecture is contracted but operationally dormant — SAP Analytics Cloud Planning is frozen at ~$3.2K/month (4.5% YTD attainment, $134K of $137K ACV unconsumed) and Document & Reporting Compliance is effectively at $0 (0.17% attainment against $9.6K ACV). Architecturally, this means the unified planning layer that should connect FP&A, workforce, and operational forecasting has never moved past pilot, and the statutory e-invoicing/VAT reporting layer that should centralise global compliance across 90+ countries has never been activated. Lilly is running finance and tax processes outside the platforms it has paid for, while the only Finance-adjacent product showing healthy adoption is Single Sign-on (86% YTD burn, trending to overage) — the identity plumbing is live, but the finance capabilities it should be securing are not.
Convene a single Procurement contract restructuring conversation within 30 days that bundles Ariba Buying & Invoicing, Ariba Sourcing, and Preferred Success Ariba into one true-up — use the $137K Sourcing overage, 5-month Buying ceiling-lock, and Preferred Success demand gap as a unified business case to right-size all three envelopes through December, and tie the Preferred Success expansion to a Jun–Dec engagement roadmap (architecture reviews, release readiness, adoption workshops) so expanded capacity is absorbed immediately rather than stalling again.
Launch a joint Finance + Tax activation program in June with Lilly's FP&A and Compliance sponsors to move SAC Planning and Document & Reporting Compliance from contracted-but-dormant to production-live — commit to one planning model go-live (budgeting, workforce, or R&D portfolio) and one statutory reporting scenario (e-invoicing or VAT) before end of Q3, supported by SAP Customer Success enablement, targeting a lift of SAC Planning to $15K+/month by August and DRC to $2K/month to recover roughly 50% of the combined $144K ACV gap.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
98.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$349K
YTD Consumed$343K
YTD ACV: $74K
Full-Year ACV: $74K · Full-Year Budget: $846K
Solution Insights
Lilly's HCM learning spine is architecturally coherent and operating as designed — SF Learning and SF Validated Learning are both showing parallel, steady attainment climbs (93.7% → 96.3%) with consumption embedded in regulated GxP and compliance workflows, indicating the LMS layer is genuinely load-bearing for Lilly's pharma training obligations rather than a shelfware deployment. The architectural gap is not capability activation but capability scope: validated curricula are clearly running, but the 4-5% persistent shortfall across both products suggests entire learner populations (contractors, external partners, manager development tracks) or adjacent use cases (onboarding, certifications, skills-to-Career-Development linkage) remain outside the LMS perimeter despite being in-contract.
The SuccessFactors platform foundation shows a two-speed adoption pattern that signals architectural underutilization at the core — HXM Operations is consuming at 38% of ACV at the 42% time mark ($3.9M of $10.27M, with a $58K cumulative gap), meaning core HR transactional processes (payroll events, time, org management) are running below designed throughput, while Preferred Success SFSF burns ratably at 100% ACV attainment regardless of whether the EAC engagements, release reviews, and CSM success planning entitlements are being consumed. Lilly is paying for the governance and enablement wrapper at full rate while the underlying platform it is meant to accelerate is the very thing lagging — the success service is architecturally disconnected from the operational adoption it should be driving.
Convene a joint Q3 learning architecture expansion review with Lilly's L&D and GxP Compliance owners to extend SF Learning and SF Validated Learning scope into 2-3 named adjacencies (clinical SOPs, manufacturing compliance refreshers, contractor/external learner enablement, onboarding automation) — target lifting monthly consumption above the June budget lines on both products to absorb the combined ~$36K/month ACV gap and convert the upward attainment trend into full FY2026 renewal justification.
Re-point the Preferred Success SFSF entitlement directly at the HXM Operations consumption gap — schedule a CSM-led utilization audit in June to inventory unused Preferred Success services, then redirect release reviews and success planning sessions onto the 2-3 underutilized HXM process areas (payroll events, time management, org transactions) with a committed run-rate target of $910K/month for Jun-Dec, so the success service produces documented operational outcomes ahead of Q4 renewal conversations.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
87.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$846K
YTD Consumed$742K
YTD ACV: $214K
Full-Year ACV: $214K · Full-Year Budget: $2.0M
Solution Insights
Lilly's Supply Chain planning backbone is architecturally hollow — Integrated Business Planning (IBP) shows a flat $143,451 monthly charge across four of five months, which is the signature of a static subscription baseline, not the variable workload pattern that real S&OP, demand sensing, and supply response cycles produce. The Plan layer is contracted but the collaborative S&OP process IBP is designed to orchestrate (demand → supply → inventory → response) is not running end-to-end; Lilly is almost certainly using one module (likely Demand) while Supply Planning, Inventory Optimization, and Response & Supply sit dormant, leaving the upstream signal that should drive Make & Deliver execution disconnected from the rest of the supply chain architecture. Compounding this, YTD consumption of $742K against an $842K YTD budget and $0 ACV booked from June onward means the planning foundation is simultaneously under-adopted and commercially uncovered for the back half of FY26.
The Make & Deliver quality release capability is architecturally abandoned — Batch Release Hub is designed to digitize GxP batch disposition, replacing paper-based release with electronic batch records and quality decision workflows, but consumption collapsed from $53 in January to $0 in April and May while $32,476/month of ACV continues to burn. At 0.08% of annual ACV realized, Lilly's quality release process is still running outside the Hub, meaning the compliance, lead-time, and inventory-carrying-cost benefits the product was contracted to deliver are not materializing, and the architectural link between manufacturing execution and regulatory submission readiness is broken. Given Lilly is a pharma manufacturer where batch release is a core GxP control point, a zeroed-out Hub signals either a validation/GxP blocker or a stalled go-live — either way, renewal defensibility is now critical.
Within 2 weeks, convene a joint session with Lilly's demand planning and S&OP leadership to map which IBP modules are actually in productive use versus licensed-but-dormant, then stand up a 90-day activation plan that brings Supply, Inventory, and Response & Supply planning online so the flat-line consumption pattern breaks and the Plan layer begins driving Make & Deliver decisions; in parallel, engage the account executive immediately to resolve the $0 ACV exposure from June onward before the commercial gap overtakes the adoption gap.
Escalate Batch Release Hub to Lilly's Quality and Manufacturing IT sponsors this week to diagnose the April shutdown — confirm whether this is a validation hold, a process-owner change, or a stalled go-live — and commit to a 60-day pilot activation at one manufacturing site to push at least one electronic batch release workflow live through the Hub, establishing a defensible consumption baseline before the Q3 budget step-up from $878 in June to $2,953 in December.
Kraft Heinz Foods Company
Consumer products
YTD Budget: $3.5M
YTD Consumed: $2.7M
YTD ACV: $1.0M
Full-Year ACV: $1.0M
Full-Year Budget: $8.6M
EA Insights
Kraft Heinz's entire SAP estate is converging on a single architectural failure mode: the integration and data fabric is collapsing precisely as the consumption layers above it accelerate. Integration Suite is eroding three months running (CPEA tier from 134% Q1 attainment to 80% in May; standalone LPR499 trending $6.8K below its $61.3K ACV), BTP Database Cloud sits at 0% while BTP Public burns at 1326% of April plan, and SAC BI has $0 consumed against $8,851 ACV — yet Joule Studio, Ariba Buying, PaPM, and Business Network are all scaling. This means every cross-domain story Kraft Heinz contracted for (AI agents fed by governed APIs, BTP workloads feeding SAC dashboards, S2P closed loop, IBP-Business Network forecast sync, Signavio model-measure-refine) is being executed on point-to-point or off-platform plumbing, which structurally caps the value of a portfolio carrying north of $3.5M in annual Business Network ACV alone and tens of thousands in dormant analytics and integration ACV.
Across Finance, Procurement, Supply Chain, and Process Transformation, Kraft Heinz exhibits the same architectural anti-pattern: the transactional/execution layer of each domain is live and scaling while the governance, intelligence, and feedback layer is dark. Ariba Buying runs at 111% but Sourcing sits at 35%, Spend Analysis went dark in April-May, and Supplier Risk is $0 against $33,710 of front-loaded ACV; S/4HANA Finance shows sawtooth $0/157% closes with PaPM exhausting ACV in a single May burst leaving $0 headroom for Jun-Dec against $828K of planned budget; Signavio Process Intelligence is mining while Process Management is dormant despite a $27.5K May ACV expansion; Business Network is transmitting POs but not running bidirectional forecast collaboration into IBP. Kraft Heinz is operating its SAP estate as disconnected systems of record rather than the systems of intelligence and control it has paid for — which directly undermines the CPG-critical objectives of margin defense through spend governance, supply resilience through supplier risk and forecast collaboration, and continuous close.
The contract-versus-consumption math across the portfolio reveals Kraft Heinz is on track to strand a material share of FY26 SAP investment while simultaneously breaching ceiling in the few products it has operationalized — a profile that destroys renewal defensibility. Business Network is projecting ~35% full-year attainment (~$2M gap against $3.15M), Signavio Process Management is tracking to ~$39K against ~$93K ACV, SAC BI is mathematically heading past the 50%-unrecoverable line, Ariba Supplier Risk and Spend Analysis are stranded, while PaPM and BTP Public have already broken the ceiling. The enterprise is paying for a complete, integrated SAP architecture but consuming a narrow execution slice of it, which means the FY27 renewal conversation will be a downsizing negotiation on the very governance and intelligence products that justify the platform premium.
Convene a single Enterprise Architecture Council session within 30 days — chaired jointly with Kraft Heinz's CIO, CFO, Chief Supply Chain Officer, and Process Excellence sponsor — to ratify one cross-domain reference architecture that explicitly names Integration Suite as the mandatory fabric for all new Joule, BTP, Ariba, Business Network, and Signavio flows, and BTP (with Database Cloud and SAC BI) as the mandatory data-and-insight tier. Tie the output to a written architectural decision record that blocks any new AI agent, iFlow retirement, or extension from being deployed off-platform, so the integration and analytics regression stops before H2 FY26.
Launch a 90-day 'governance layer activation' program bundling the dormant intelligence and control products into named business outcomes: Supplier Risk + Spend Analysis + Sourcing reactivated on 3-5 anchor categories before Q3 sourcing awards; Process Management onboarded modelers tied to S/4HANA or M&A workstreams consuming the May $27.5K ACV uplift; SAC BI go-live on one finance or supply chain dashboard sourced from existing BTP workloads; Business Network forecast collaboration and ASN flows activated in monthly waves tied to IBP cycles. Each thread closes a specific cross-domain loop and collectively recovers the ~$2M+ in projected stranded ACV.
Rebaseline the FY26 commercial curve with Kraft Heinz before end of Q3: open a true-forward conversation on PaPM and BTP Public (both projected to exhaust ceiling), re-phase Joule Studio and Technology Foundation Other to actual run rates, and pair every uplift with a contractually-linked re-acceleration plan for Integration Suite (targeting $5,090/month LPR499 and $15.4K/month CPEA-metered tier on B2B/EDI and S/4 event flows), Business Network ($130K+/month), and Signavio Process Management ($5,700+/month). This converts the current 'overrun-and-shelfware' pattern into a balanced consumption profile that protects the FY27 renewal narrative for the full architectural footprint.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
4.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$4K
YTD Consumed$188
YTD ACV: $2K
Full-Year ACV: $2K · Full-Year Budget: $9K
Solution Insights
The Data & AI foundation at Kraft Heinz is architecturally incoherent: BTP (Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement) Public consumption is running 3-13x over plan while the Database Cloud variant of the same agreement sits at 0% — meaning compute and application services are being consumed aggressively without a corresponding persistence/data tier being activated. This signals workloads are running on BTP Public against external or non-SAP data stores, bypassing the intended SAP-managed data layer and fragmenting the Data & AI architecture Kraft Heinz contracted for.
The analytics consumption layer is contractually present but operationally absent — SAP Analytics Cloud BI has $0 consumed against a $5,127 front-loaded Q1 ACV and zero activation since January, while BTP Public is burning compute at 1326% of April plan. Kraft Heinz is generating and processing data on the platform but has no sanctioned visualization or decision-making surface live, meaning business users have no governed path from BTP data workloads to insight — the Data & AI investment is producing pipelines without consumers.
Convene a joint BTP consumption and SAC BI activation review within 30 days: identify which workloads drove the Feb-Apr BTP Public overage, determine whether they require Database Cloud services (to break the 0% pattern there), and connect at least one of those datasets to SAC BI as the first activation use case — this simultaneously rebaselines the overconsuming product, justifies the dormant database tier, and unblocks the stalled analytics layer with one architectural decision.
Launch a 60-day SAC BI go-live sprint anchored to a single Kraft Heinz business-unit dashboard (finance or supply chain) sourced from existing BTP workloads, with explicit milestones for tenant provisioning, SAP BW/S4 connectivity, and first user login by end of June — without activation this month, more than 50% of the $8,851 annual SAC BI budget becomes mathematically unrecoverable and the Data & AI stack remains a compute-only deployment.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
104.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$112K
YTD Consumed$118K
YTD ACV: $5K
Full-Year ACV: $5K · Full-Year Budget: $273K
Solution Insights
Kraft Heinz's Technology Foundation is bifurcating into two divergent adoption stories on the same Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement: Joule Studio and general platform workloads are accelerating hard (Joule Studio at $128–154/month against near-zero plan, projecting ~$1,543 vs $709 budget; Technology Foundation Other at 144% and 136% the last two months), while Integration Suite — the architectural backbone that exposes APIs and iFlows to those very AI and platform workloads — is eroding three months running ($5,030 → $4,328, with standalone LPR499 trending to leave ~$4,800 of ACV unused). Architecturally this is incoherent: the LoB is scaling AI and extension consumption on Joule and BTP without scaling the integration fabric that is supposed to feed them governed, AI-ready APIs and event flows, which means new agents and extensions are likely being wired through point-to-point or non-SAP middleware and the 'integrate AI agents, apps, and data with one solution' value of Integration Suite is being bypassed.
The Integration Suite decline is a capability regression, not a cost story — at the CPEA-metered tier consumption fell from 134% attainment in Q1 to 80% in May ($12.7K) and the standalone Integration Suite contract is trending $6.8K below its $61.3K ACV, which in a CPG operation of Kraft Heinz's scale signals retired iFlows or B2B/EDI trading-partner and S/4HANA event flows being decommissioned or migrated off-platform rather than optimized. With Joule Studio ramping in parallel, the LoB is heading into the second half of FY26 with more AI surface area and less governed integration underneath it, which puts process reliability, API governance, and the renewal narrative for Integration Suite all at risk simultaneously.
Convene a single joint architecture review with Kraft Heinz's integration lead and BTP/Joule platform owner within two weeks to map every iFlow retired or migrated since March against the Joule Studio and Technology Foundation Other workloads that surged in April–May — the goal is to confirm whether new AI agents and extensions are being built directly on S/4 or third-party middleware bypassing Integration Suite, and to re-anchor net-new Joule and BTP use cases onto Integration Suite APIs so the integration fabric scales with the AI layer it is meant to serve.
Rebaseline the FY2026 CPEA budget curve and open a commercial true-forward conversation before end of Q3: re-phase the Joule Studio plan to reflect the ~$140/month actual run rate (projecting ~$1,543 vs $709), size Technology Foundation Other against the $2,177/month trajectory (projecting ~$4K overage), and pair that with a 7-month Integration Suite re-acceleration plan targeting $5,090/month on LPR499 and $15.4K/month on the CPEA-metered tier, anchored on B2B/EDI and event-driven S/4HANA flows so the recovered consumption restores the architectural role Integration Suite is contracted to play.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
55.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$308K
YTD Consumed$172K
YTD ACV: $114K
Full-Year ACV: $114K · Full-Year Budget: $750K
Solution Insights
