The Signavio process transformation stack is architecturally split-brained at Kraft Heinz: Process Management (the modeling and governance layer where target processes are designed) and Process Intelligence (the mining and conformance layer where actual execution is measured) should operate as a closed loop — model in Management, measure in Intelligence, refine in Management. The signals show Intelligence was ramping steadily through April with real mining workloads, while Management consumption is flatlining at ~$3.2K/month despite a $27.5K ACV expansion in May. The LoB is investing in seeing how processes actually run but not in redesigning them — meaning insights from Process Intelligence have nowhere to land architecturally, and the transformation flywheel is broken.
Process Management is contractually expanded but operationally dormant — the May ACV jump to $46,471 against just $2,944 of consumption indicates Kraft Heinz has bought capacity for a broader modeling footprint (likely tied to an S/4HANA transformation, M&A integration, or new BU rollout) without standing up the modeler community, governance hubs, or publication workflows needed to consume it. With only 17.5% of full-year ACV consumed and 7 months remaining, the current run rate projects ~$39K year-end consumption against ~$93K ACV, leaving more than half the investment stranded and putting renewal defensibility at risk.
Reconcile the -$71,055 May Process Intelligence entry in writing with the customer within 2 weeks and republish a corrected YTD attainment, then immediately convene a process-scope expansion workshop to onboard 2-3 new mining scopes (O2C, P2P, S2P) — this protects the Q1 adoption narrative and converts the ~$401K remaining Intelligence runway into sustained mining workloads before executive confidence erodes.
Launch a 90-day joint enablement sprint with the Kraft Heinz process excellence sponsor to tie the $27,550 May Process Management uplift to a named transformation workstream (S/4HANA, M&A, or BU rollout), targeting ≥$5,700/month consumption via modeler onboarding, collaboration hub publication, and an explicit handoff loop from Process Intelligence findings into Process Management redesigns — this closes the model-measure-refine architectural gap and prevents the ACV expansion from becoming shelfware.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
87.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$2.3M
YTD Consumed$2.0M
YTD ACV: $627K
Full-Year ACV: $627K · Full-Year Budget: $5.4M
Solution Insights
The Procure-to-Pay backbone at Kraft Heinz is architecturally lopsided — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is running at 111% budget attainment and embedded in daily AP operations, but the upstream and governance layers are collapsing: Ariba Sourcing is stuck at ~35% attainment with a flat $2.1K–$2.8K monthly ceiling, Ariba Spend Analysis went dark in April–May (12.6% YTD), and Ariba Supplier Risk has never activated ($0 across five months against $33,710 of front-loaded ACV). The LoB is transacting purchases at scale without the sourcing rigor, spend visibility, or supplier risk monitoring that the contracted architecture was designed to wrap around it — meaning Kraft Heinz is paying invoices efficiently but sourcing blindly and onboarding suppliers without risk gates.
The Finance close and profitability stack is operating in burst mode rather than as a continuous-close architecture. S/4HANA Finance Cloud — designed for real-time universal journal and continuous close — shows a sawtooth pattern with a $0 February and a 157% May spike, indicating users are reverting to legacy tooling between catch-up runs. Profitability and Performance Management compounds this: it sat dormant Jan–Mar, then burned 183% of monthly ACV in May, exhausting contract ceiling with $828K of planned budget still ahead and $0 ACV headroom for Jun–Dec. The intended architecture (S/4HANA Finance feeding PaPM for granular P&L steering) is technically live but operationally desynchronized from the monthly close calendar, so the LoB is getting episodic insight instead of the continuous financial steering the stack was contracted to deliver.
Re-anchor the Ariba procurement suite as one integrated S2P process: convene a single executive review with Kraft Heinz procurement leadership covering Sourcing, Spend Analysis, and Supplier Risk together — commit to a Q3 activation plan that (a) moves 3–5 new spend categories into Sourcing events, (b) reactivates Spend Analysis on one anchor category to inform those events, and (c) loads the tier-1 supplier population into Supplier Risk before sourcing awards are issued, so Buying and Invoicing volume is governed by the upstream controls already paid for.
Lock S/4HANA Finance Cloud and PaPM into the monthly close calendar with the Kraft Heinz finance systems owner — schedule journal, intercompany, reconciliation, and allocation runs on fixed dates each month to eliminate $0 months and convert PaPM's May burst into a recurring run rate; in parallel, open a contract capacity conversation now to true-up PaPM ACV before the $0 Jun–Dec headroom throttles the profitability use cases just as adoption is taking hold.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
55.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$830K
YTD Consumed$457K
YTD ACV: $263K
Full-Year ACV: $263K · Full-Year Budget: $2.2M
Solution Insights
Kraft Heinz has deployed SAP Business Network Supply Chain Collaboration as the connective tissue between their S/4HANA and IBP environments and their supplier base, but the bursty consumption pattern (March $75K → April $143K → May $125K) reveals the platform is being used as a project-based onboarding tool rather than the steady-state collaboration backbone it was architected to be. Forecast collaboration, PO collaboration, and inventory visibility are designed to run as continuous signal exchanges with suppliers — the volatility indicates that only a subset of document flows are live and supplier activation is happening in episodic waves, leaving the resiliency and transparency capabilities structurally underutilized.
The YTD trajectory of $456K against a $3.15M annual ceiling — projecting to ~35% full-year attainment — tells us the architectural promise of the Business Network (value scaling with transaction volume across the supplier base) is not being realized because Kraft Heinz is likely running a narrow set of transaction types with a narrow slice of suppliers. For a CPG manufacturer of this scale, this consumption profile is inconsistent with end-to-end procurement and forecast collaboration being operational; it suggests the network is functioning as a PO transmission channel rather than a bidirectional demand-supply synchronization layer feeding IBP.
Commission a transaction-type audit with Kraft Heinz supply chain and procurement leads to map which Business Network document flows (POs, order confirmations, ASNs, invoices, forecast commits, quality notifications) are actually live versus contracted-but-dormant, then sequence activation of 2-3 dormant flows per quarter to convert the contract into continuous collaboration and close the projected $2M attainment gap.
Replicate the April supplier onboarding wave as a repeatable monthly cadence tied to IBP forecast cycles — institutionalizing 1-2 supplier enablement waves per month would both stabilize consumption above the $130K monthly threshold needed to reach 90%+ attainment and operationalize the forecast collaboration capability that justifies the Business Network investment in the first place.
Marathon Petroleum Company LP
Oil, gas, and energy
YTD Budget: $805K
YTD Consumed: $767K
YTD ACV: $222K
Full-Year ACV: $222K
Full-Year Budget: $2.0M
EA Insights
Marathon has contracted a coherent SAP platform spanning S/4HANA Private Cloud, Ariba P2P, Signavio, SAC Planning, and Joule — but the BTP integration and data fabric backbone meant to bind them is architecturally dormant: BDC Public sits at zero, the BTP EA Integration Suite line went dormant after a single January spike, the Finance BTP contract has zero consumption, and the standalone Integration Suite is being throttled down. The enterprise is operating as five disconnected product islands rather than the closed-loop digital core it was bought as, which directly blocks Marathon's refining-sector imperative of margin optimization through real-time spend, operations, and financial signal integration.
Across every L1 domain the same anti-pattern repeats — the transactional/runtime layer is over-consuming while the governance, design, and intelligence layer is starving: Database Cloud at 537% vs BDC at 0%, Joule Studio at 320% vs Integration Suite dormant, Ariba Buying at 138% vs Catalog at 35%, and S/4HANA Private Cloud running without Preferred Success. Marathon is industrializing execution faster than it is industrializing control, meaning every domain is accruing compliance, architectural drift, and off-contract spend risk simultaneously — a systemic posture issue, not four isolated consumption problems.
The commercial envelope is materially mis-sized against the architecture in flight — Database Cloud projects ~$108K overage, Ariba Buying ~$155K overage, and Joule Studio is running 3.2x plan, while BDC Public, BTP Foundation Other, Finance BTP, Ariba Catalog, and Preferred Success will collectively strand 35–100% of their entitlements. Marathon will exit FY2026 having overpaid for runtime and underutilized the very capabilities (data products, integration, catalog compliance, success advisory) that convert SAP from infrastructure into business outcomes, undermining the FY2027 renewal narrative.
Within 30 days, convene a Chief Architect-level Enterprise Integration & Data Fabric review with Marathon that treats BTP, Integration Suite, and BDC as one cross-domain backbone — map each over-consuming workload (Joule agents, Ariba Buying, Database Cloud, Signavio Process Intelligence findings, SAC Planning models) to the specific APIs, events, and governed data products it requires, and commit a sequenced Jun–Sep activation plan with named business owners so the dormant platform layer comes online in step with the workloads already in production.
Run a single consolidated FY2026 commercial true-up and FY2027 baseline reset in the next 45 days that addresses Database Cloud, Ariba Buying & Invoicing, and Joule Studio overages as one package, funded in part by repurposing the structurally underutilized lines (BDC Public, BTP Other, Finance BTP, Ariba Catalog headroom, Preferred Success) — preventing mid-year service constraints while re-anchoring the contract shape to the customer's true consumption architecture.
Launch a 'governance and control layer' rescue program within 14 days covering the three starved control points — Ariba Catalog re-enablement, Preferred Success sponsor re-alignment for S/4HANA Private Cloud, and Signavio Process Management CoE cadence linked to Process Intelligence findings — packaged as one executive conversation with Marathon's CFO/COO sponsors, since each gap individually erodes margin discipline but together they signal the enterprise is running execution platforms without their governance scaffolding.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
1024.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$5K
YTD Consumed$49K
YTD ACV: $0
Full-Year ACV: $0 · Full-Year Budget: $12K
Solution Insights
Marathon's BAIP Data & AI footprint shows a split-personality adoption pattern on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement: the Database Cloud entitlement is the de facto production platform — absorbing real workloads at ~$9.8K/month and already at 537% of annual plan — while the BDC Public entitlement, which is the intended home for the broader Business Data Cloud and AI-ready data fabric capabilities, sits at absolute zero. Architecturally this means Marathon is consuming raw database/runtime capacity but has not activated the higher-value BDC data products, governed sharing, and AI grounding layer that BAIP is designed to deliver, so the LoB is paying for database compute without the data & AI capability stack on top.
The commercial envelope is fundamentally mis-sized against the architecture in flight: $49.2K consumed against a $3.1K YTD / $9.2K annual Database Cloud budget signals an unplanned production workload running on enterprise agreement terms, while the parallel $1,679 YTD / $3,200 annual BDC Public budget is back-loaded into Aug–Sep with no activation path in sight. Left unaddressed, Marathon ends FY2026 with a ~$108K overage on one entitlement and ~36% underattainment on the other — a clear signal that the EA was contracted as a balanced Data & AI platform but is being consumed as a single-purpose database, leaving the data-to-AI value chain architecturally incomplete.
Within two weeks, run a joint workload and architecture reconciliation with Marathon's data platform owner and procurement: identify which services drove the Database Cloud spike (especially the May $13.5K), classify them as production vs. sprawl, and initiate a true-up or expanded commercial construct sized to the demonstrated ~$10K/month run rate before the gap compounds over the remaining 7 months.
Launch a 30-day BDC Public activation plan tied to a concrete first use case that extends the existing Database Cloud workload — e.g., exposing those datasets as governed BDC data products for SAC or AI consumption — and sequence milestones into the Jun–Sep budget peaks so the BDC capability layer comes online in step with where Marathon's data already lives, converting the EA from a database line item into a true Data & AI platform.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
167.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$107K
YTD Consumed$179K
YTD ACV: $26K
Full-Year ACV: $26K · Full-Year Budget: $264K
Solution Insights
The Technology Foundation for Marathon is architecturally bifurcated: Joule Studio on the BTP Enterprise Agreement is running at 320% of annual budget through May ($79K consumed vs $24.7K planned), while the standalone Integration Suite contract (LPR499) is being actively throttled — consumption dropped from 108% to 95% of budget the exact month the ACV stepped up from $19.5K to $25.7K. This tells us the LoB is aggressively adopting AI/agent tooling but is not yet generating the integration workloads needed to feed those agents with the 'secure, reliable APIs optimized for AI consumption' that Integration Suite is architecturally meant to deliver — the AI layer is outrunning its own data and process backbone.
The BTP foundation itself is fragmented and largely dormant outside of Joule: the Integration Suite line on the BTP EA spiked once in January ($1,800) then went dormant with a credit reversal in May, and the Technology Foundation Other line shows $0 consumption across all five months against a $931 cumulative budget. Combined with the standalone Integration Suite being managed-down rather than expanded, this signals Marathon has provisioned a broad BTP surface area but only operationalized one capability (Joule Studio) — the extension, eventing, and governance capabilities that make Integration Suite valuable for agent-ready APIs are contracted but not architecturally live.
Convene a 30-day BTP architecture alignment workshop that ties the Joule Studio overconsumption directly to Integration Suite use cases — every AI agent built in Joule should be mapped to the APIs, events, and integration flows it depends on, converting the standalone Integration Suite's unused April–May headroom and the dormant BTP EA Integration Suite line into the data plane for the agents already in production.
Restructure the FY2027 BTP commercial baseline now: right-size Joule Studio upward to reflect the ~$13K/month run-rate, retire or repurpose the $931 Technology Foundation Other line that has never activated, and re-anchor the standalone Integration Suite expansion against a committed pipeline of 2–3 new integration scenarios (B2B onboarding, event-driven flows, API-managed external integrations) with named business owners and monthly consumption milestones tracked in QBRs.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
61.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$158K
YTD Consumed$97K
YTD ACV: $47K
Full-Year ACV: $47K · Full-Year Budget: $384K
Solution Insights
The Signavio transformation stack at Marathon is architecturally lopsided — Process Intelligence (the mining/discovery layer) carries the bulk of the investment with ~$37K ACV while Process Management (the modeling/governance layer) sits at only $15K, yet Management just absorbed a 3.7x ACV uplift in May with no matching modeling activity. This signals an intent to operationalize discovered insights into governed to-be processes, but without a connected handoff from Intelligence findings into Management workflows, the LoB is buying the 'design' capability before proving it can consume the 'discover' capability — the two products are contracted as a closed loop but are being adopted as disconnected tools.
Process Intelligence adoption is decelerating architecturally, not just financially — the Jan-Feb over-burn followed by Mar-Apr decline to sub-95% indicates the initial process-mining use cases (likely a finite set of O2C or P2P discovery projects) have completed without a next-wave backlog, and the -$29,700 May entry is a billing artifact masking that the analyst community has effectively run out of scoped work. Without a standing pipeline of mining scopes tied to refinery operations or finance processes, the Intelligence platform will continue to drift from a transformation engine into a one-time assessment tool.
Reconcile the -$29,700 May billing entry on Process Intelligence within two weeks and, in parallel, commission a use-case expansion workshop to queue 2-3 new mining scopes (refinery maintenance, P2P, or close-cycle processes) — this both restates the true $67K ACV gap for steering committee accuracy and rebuilds the analyst consumption pipeline needed to absorb the remaining ~$220K of forward budget.
Tie the May Process Management ACV expansion to a named center-of-excellence cadence within 30 days — assign business process owners to specific models discovered via Process Intelligence, set a monthly modeling rhythm targeting at least $760/month, and explicitly link each modeling initiative to an upstream Intelligence finding so the two products operate as the intended discover-to-design loop rather than parallel point tools.
Solution Area
Cloud ERP Private
38.3%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$23K
YTD Consumed$9K
YTD ACV: $5K
Full-Year ACV: $5K · Full-Year Budget: $55K
Solution Insights
Marathon's Cloud ERP Private estate is running without its success scaffolding — Preferred Success for S/4HANA Private Cloud is the entitled channel for release advisory, continuous quality checks, and CoE enablement, and the Jan–Mar plateau followed by an April step-down indicates the LoB has architecturally decoupled the platform from its guided-adoption layer. Operating S/4HANA Private Cloud without active Preferred Success consumption means upgrade readiness, technical governance, and innovation adoption are now reactive rather than orchestrated, exposing the core ERP to drift and unrealized roadmap value.
The consumption pattern signals a sponsorship or value-articulation breakdown rather than a capacity issue — at 23.8% May attainment and a projected ~30% FY2026 utilization, roughly 70% of the entitled expert sessions, remediation guidance, and release planning services will be stranded. This leaves Marathon's S/4HANA Private Cloud lifecycle (testing, patching, innovation uptake) unsupported by SAP-led advisory for the remaining seven months, increasing the risk that the private cloud platform is treated as commodity hosting instead of a managed business capability.
Within 14 days, convene a Preferred Success replanning workshop with Marathon's S/4HANA Private Cloud owner to map specific entitlements (release advisory, CQCs, expert sessions) to named FY2026 business outcomes and rebuild a Jun–Dec consumption calendar targeting at least $3,000/month to close the $3,454 May budget gap.
Escalate the April drop-off internally and run a sponsor re-alignment call to confirm whether the original Preferred Success executive sponsor at Marathon is still active; without re-anchoring sponsorship, the remaining $4,548–$4,653 monthly budget will continue to erode and the S/4HANA Private Cloud platform will remain without its governance layer.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
84.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$513K
YTD Consumed$433K
YTD ACV: $144K
Full-Year ACV: $144K · Full-Year Budget: $1.2M
Solution Insights
Marathon's Procure-to-Pay architecture is operationally inverted — Ariba Buying and Invoicing (the transactional engine for PO-to-payment automation and three-way matching) is consuming at 122% YTD attainment and accelerating to 138.7% in May, while Ariba Catalog (the upstream control point that routes buyers to preferred suppliers and negotiated prices) has decayed 35% from January and sits at 35.5% YTD attainment. The LoB is processing massive transaction volume without the catalog-driven compliance layer that's supposed to feed it, meaning Marathon is automating invoices for spend that is increasingly going off-contract — the exact opposite of the architectural intent, and a direct driver of the runaway invoicing consumption.
The Finance & Spend Management analytics and planning stack is fragmented and under-leveraged — SAC Planning is locked at a $3,833/month ceiling indicating a single recurring planning model rather than the unified financial-and-operational planning the product is architected to deliver, while the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (the extension and integration foundation that would connect SAC Planning to Ariba spend data and S/4HANA actuals) sits at zero consumption five months in. Without BTP activated, SAC Planning cannot evolve beyond its isolated workload, and Marathon has no architectural path to driver-based planning informed by the live procurement signals Ariba is generating.
Convene a joint Procurement and AP architecture review in the next 30 days to rebalance the P2P stack: diagnose why Catalog consumption is declining (stale content, supplier enablement gaps, punchout coverage) and tie Catalog re-activation directly to the Ariba Buying overage story — every dollar routed through Catalog reduces uncontrolled invoice volume. Target $9K+/month Catalog consumption while simultaneously opening a contract true-up conversation on Buying and Invoicing to address the projected $155K full-year overage before mid-year service constraints hit.
Launch a BTP activation workshop in June pairing Marathon's platform owner with the SAC Planning team to define 1–2 integration use cases that connect Ariba spend actuals and S/4HANA financials into SAC Planning — this simultaneously unblocks the dormant $339 BTP contract and breaks SAC Planning out of its $3,833 single-model ceiling by enabling driver-based financial planning. Sequence Sourcing event expansion (targeting new category managers to lift the flat $15.9K/month run rate) into the same roadmap so sourcing outcomes feed the same planning models.
S.C. Johnson & Son, Inc.
Consumer products
YTD Budget: $735K
YTD Consumed: $600K
YTD ACV: $214K
Full-Year ACV: $214K
Full-Year Budget: $1.8M
EA Insights
S.C. Johnson has systematically purchased the value layers of SAP's architecture (BDC analytics, Concur T&E suite, Blackline close, Business Network collaboration, Integrated Product Development) while leaving the underlying Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement at effectively 0% consumption across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance — the BTP runtime that is architecturally assumed to host extensions, integrations, and AI agents simply does not exist as a live environment. This is a classic 'inverted pyramid' posture: the insight and process layers are running on borrowed foundation, meaning every cross-domain story (Concur↔S/4↔Blackline reconciliation, BDC↔Database Cloud persistence, PLM↔Supply Chain feasibility) is being delivered via point integrations or manual workarounds that will collapse under scale or the next core upgrade.
Integration Suite is contracting (-58% on standalone, -11% on bundled) at exactly the moment when three independent L1 domains require it as connective tissue — BDC needs governed pipelines to Database Cloud, Finance needs Concur/Blackline/S4 reconciliation flows, and Supply Chain needs trading-partner and PLM-to-manufacturing bridges. The decline is not a domain problem, it is an enterprise architecture problem: without Integration Suite operating as the standard governed backbone, the Business Network's frozen $48.6K monthly drawdown, IPD's 5.7% attainment, and the Blackline $75.7K ACV gap are all locked in place because none of them can be activated without the integration layer they depend on.
The portfolio shows a consistent pattern of contracted-but-dormant 'bridge' capabilities that connect S.C. Johnson's CPG operating model end-to-end — IPD (R&D→Supply Chain) at 5.7%, Business Network Collaboration flat at supplier-onboarding standstill, CPEA at near-zero, and Direct Travel/Analytics/Triplink decoupled from a collapsing Concur spine. Annualized, roughly $500K+ of contracted ACV across these bridges is producing little to no architectural value, while the consumption layer (BDC at 4-5x ceiling, ExpenseIt at intended capacity) is over-running. For a CPG company whose competitive advantage depends on rapid reformulation, supplier resiliency, and integrated spend visibility, this means the enterprise is investing in SAP's vision of a connected value chain but operating as a collection of disconnected islands.
Within 30 days, commission a single Enterprise Architecture Review with S.C. Johnson's CIO, Chief Data Officer, and platform architects that consolidates the three dormant-foundation problems (CPEA across BAIP and Finance, Integration Suite decline, Database Cloud/BDC Public zero-consumption) into one 'Platform Foundation Activation' roadmap — land 2-3 anchor workloads (a Blackline↔S/4 reconciliation iFlow, a BDC↔Database Cloud data product, and a Joule Studio agent) before end of Q2 FY2026 so the foundation exists before the BDC true-up and Business Network onboarding sprints depend on it.
Run a cross-L1 'Connected Value Chain' workshop in June pairing R&D/PLM, Supply Chain, and Finance leaders to sequence IPD activation against a concrete NPI/reformulation project, with Business Network supplier onboarding waves and Concur/Blackline finance integration tied to the same launch milestone — this converts four separately stalled domains into one CPG-relevant business narrative (faster reformulation → integrated supplier ramp → automated cost/close visibility) and forces Integration Suite to be the shared backbone rather than a tactical tool.
Within 2 weeks, treat the Concur Expense ACV collapse ($19.9K→$5.5K) and the BDC over-consumption ($46.8K YTD vs $30.6K ACV) as a single commercial-architecture event with procurement: rebalance the contract so the over-running value layers (BDC, ExpenseIt) are right-sized upward while the dormant foundation and bridge assets (CPEA, Database Cloud, BDC Public, IPD, Business Network) are either reprofiled to realistic ramps tied to the activation roadmap above, or de-scoped — preventing FY2026 from closing with simultaneous overage exposure and ~$500K of forfeited foundation ACV.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
189.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$25K
YTD Consumed$47K
YTD ACV: $31K
Full-Year ACV: $31K · Full-Year Budget: $73K
Solution Insights
The Data & AI architecture for S.C. Johnson is bifurcated: SAP Business Data Cloud — the SaaS fabric meant to unify mission-critical data, power SAC/Datasphere, and anchor agentic AI — has clearly hit product-market fit internally, while the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (covering BDC Public and Database Cloud) sits at zero consumption five months in. This means the LoB is consuming the analytics and AI-ready data layer aggressively but has not activated the underlying platform and database runtime that are architecturally intended to host extensions, integrations, and persistent data workloads supporting BDC at scale — a fragile pattern where the value layer is racing ahead of its foundation.
The commercial and architectural risk is now concentrated in BDC: YTD consumption of $46,784 has already breached the $30,579 annual ACV in May, with the May run rate ($15.2K) trending to 4–5x contracted ceiling by December. Combined with $1,345 of fully dormant CPEA budget (BDC Public + Database Cloud) front-loaded in Q1 and spiking again in December ($409 combined), the LoB is simultaneously over-consuming the insight layer and forfeiting the platform layer — signaling that data products and AI use cases are being delivered without the governed platform substrate (extensibility, database services) that SAP's reference architecture assumes.
Within 2 weeks, convene a joint commercial and architecture review with S.C. Johnson's procurement and data platform owner: present the BDC trajectory ($4.8K → $15.2K monthly), true-up or expand the BDC ACV to protect adoption momentum, and in the same conversation reposition the dormant CPEA (BDC Public + Database Cloud) as the foundational layer for the workloads already driving BDC growth — converting two separate problems into one consolidated Data & AI platform expansion narrative.
Run a workload discovery and activation workshop in the next 30 days to (a) document the specific data products, users, and source connections fueling the BDC May spike, and (b) map 1–2 of those workloads onto Database Cloud and BDC Public so the $256 Nov–Dec Database Cloud budget and $247 Nov–Dec BDC Public budget land on real usage — ensuring the December budget milestones reflect an integrated architecture rather than another zero-attainment month.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
70.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$26K
YTD Consumed$19K
YTD ACV: $4K
Full-Year ACV: $4K · Full-Year Budget: $84K
Solution Insights
The Technology Foundation is architecturally contracted but functionally inert — the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement, which should serve as the runtime substrate for extensions, integrations, and AI-ready services, shows $83 consumed against $1,407 budget on Joule Studio (5.9%) and $0 against $368 on the broader BTP footprint. S.C. Johnson holds the platform license but has not landed workloads on it, meaning the foundation that other SAP investments are supposed to extend and integrate through simply does not exist as a live environment today.
Integration Suite is being used as a point-to-point tactical tool rather than the governed, AI-ready integration backbone it is architected to be — consumption declined 58% from $456 in March to $191 in May on the standalone SKU, and the BTP-bundled Integration Suite line dropped 11% to $3,164/month. Architecturally, this means iFlows are being built for isolated projects but the platform is not embedded as S.C. Johnson's standard for API governance, B2B/EDI, or event-driven integration — leaving the 'integrate AI agents, apps, and data with one solution' value proposition unrealized and projecting a ~51% annual attainment shortfall of ~$37K on the CPEA.
Convene a joint BTP activation workshop within 30 days with S.C. Johnson's platform architects to land one production workload (extension, data service, or Joule Studio agent) on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement before end of Q2 FY2026 — without a live workload anchoring the platform, the Integration Suite and Joule Studio capabilities have no architectural runtime to expand into, and the $1,324 + $368 cumulative gaps will continue compounding.
Run an integration use-case discovery with S.C. Johnson's integration architects to onboard 2–3 net-new iFlows in June–July (S/4HANA-to-non-SAP, B2B/EDI, or event-driven), and investigate what drove the March $456 spike on LPR499 to replicate it as a repeatable adoption pattern — this reframes Integration Suite from isolated point integrations into the standard governed integration layer and bends the consumption curve before the H2 budget ramp on the bundled CPEA line.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
90.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$333K
YTD Consumed$300K
YTD ACV: $90K
Full-Year ACV: $90K · Full-Year Budget: $810K
Solution Insights
The T&E architecture at S.C. Johnson is broadly deployed but structurally underutilized — Concur Expense (the system of record for reimbursement), Direct Travel (booking), Analytics (spend visibility), Triplink (itinerary capture), and ExpenseIt (receipt OCR) are all active, yet only ExpenseIt is operating at intended capacity. The May collapse in Concur Expense to 21.3% attainment alongside a sharp ACV step-down from $19,917 to $5,489 is the critical signal: if the core expense engine is contracting, the surrounding Analytics ($116 ACV gap), Triplink ($165 gap), and Direct Travel ($5,141 ACV gap) capabilities lose their upstream transaction volume, meaning the LoB risks owning a full T&E suite that produces diminishing insight because the transactional spine is weakening.
The financial close and platform foundation layers are misaligned with the T&E investment — Blackline is delivering close automation at 92.4% budget attainment but leaving $75,703 of YTD ACV unused (reconciliation scope is narrower than contracted), while Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement sits at zero consumption, meaning the BTP extensibility layer that would normally integrate Concur, Blackline, and S/4HANA finance data is dormant. The LoB has bought the close-acceleration and platform-integration capabilities but is operating Blackline as a point tool rather than as part of an integrated finance architecture.
Treat the Concur Expense May drop as a P0 architectural event: validate within 2 weeks whether the ACV step-down to $5,489 reflects a license true-down or a data error, then rebuild the T&E adoption plan top-down from Expense transaction volume — because Direct Travel, Analytics, and Triplink ACV gaps cannot be closed until the Expense spine is restored to steady-state submission volume.
Activate Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement within 60 days by scoping a Blackline-to-S/4HANA reconciliation integration or a Concur-to-finance data pipeline as the first BTP workload — this simultaneously addresses the zero-consumption BTP risk and unlocks the $75,703 Blackline ACV gap by expanding reconciliation scope to additional entities through automated data flows rather than manual onboarding.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
66.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$351K
YTD Consumed$234K
YTD ACV: $89K
Full-Year ACV: $89K · Full-Year Budget: $846K
Solution Insights
The Supply Chain Collaboration backbone is contracted but operationally frozen — Business Network Supply Chain Collaboration is the architectural connector that should synchronize forecasts, POs, and ASNs between S.C. Johnson's S/4HANA/IBP core and its trading partner ecosystem, yet the identical $48,658 monthly drawdown for four months followed by a May drop to $34,803 confirms no new suppliers, document types, or forecast collaboration flows are being activated. The LoB is paying for a multi-enterprise collaboration fabric while continuing to transact with suppliers outside it, which means supply chain resiliency and upstream visibility — the core reasons this product exists — are not being realized despite a $229K YTD spend trending $170K below plan.
The new product introduction bridge is effectively severed — SAP Integrated Product Development is architected to connect R&D, PLM, manufacturing, and procurement so that formulations and specifications flow into supply chain feasibility and cost decisions, but two consecutive months at $0 consumption and only 5.7% YTD budget attainment ($4,801 of $84,512) show the platform is not embedded in any active S.C. Johnson innovation workflow. For a CPG manufacturer whose competitive cycle depends on reformulation and rapid launches, this dormancy means product engineering decisions are being made disconnected from supply chain and cost data, undermining the upstream half of the Make & Deliver architecture and putting ~90% of remaining ACV at risk.
Launch a joint trading-partner onboarding sprint with S.C. Johnson supply chain and IT sponsors in June, sequencing PO, ASN, and invoice flows for the top unintegrated suppliers and tying each wave to a month-by-month ramp that consumes the $413,806 of remaining Business Network budget rather than letting the flat subscription drawdown persist.
Convene an executive business review with R&D and PLM leadership within two weeks to anchor SAP Integrated Product Development to one concrete reformulation or NPI project, then co-build a 7-month activation roadmap targeting at least $15,287/month starting in June so the PLM-to-supply-chain bridge becomes operational against a tangible launch milestone.
Stepan Company
Chemicals
YTD Budget: $672K
YTD Consumed: $506K
YTD ACV: $145K
Full-Year ACV: $145K
Full-Year Budget: $1.7M
EA Insights
Stepan's entire SAP architecture is suffering from a single root-cause pattern: the platform substrate is dormant while the application layers above it erode. BTP Integration Suite is down 17% Jan→May and Joule Studio is flat-lining at ~$580/month, while SAP Business Data Cloud and CPEA sit at $0 consumed — meaning the integration fabric and AI/data foundation that should connect HCM's Hire-to-Retire chain, Procurement's Source-to-Pay chain, and any cross-domain analytics simply do not exist in an active state. This is why every downstream domain shows the same signature — disconnected islands of consumption rather than end-to-end process flows — and it forecloses Stepan's ability to deliver the integrated specialty-chemicals operating model (connected procurement, workforce, and operational analytics) that justifies the SAP investment in the first place.
The integration breakage is visible identically in two independent domains, confirming it is architectural rather than domain-specific: in HCM, Recruiting ($3,938/mo flat), Onboarding ($348/mo declining), EC (91.8% and sliding), and EC Payroll (narrow $4,759–$4,906 band) operate as four disconnected islands instead of one Hire-to-Retire chain; in Procurement, Sourcing (13.9%), Catalog (0.5%), Buying & Invoicing (63.7%), and Concur Expense (82% May collapse) show the same three-of-four-links-broken pattern across Source-to-Pay. Without Integration Suite actively wiring these chains together and without BDC providing a semantic layer for cross-process visibility, Stepan cannot close the loops that drive compliance, cost control, or workforce productivity — the contracted business outcomes are architecturally unreachable regardless of how much adoption work is done inside each module.
Across all four L1 areas Stepan has consistently purchased the full architectural stack but is consuming only the peripheral, low-risk layers — Concur's analytics/reporting modules run >110% while the transactional Expense core collapses; HCM's Preferred Success entitlement straight-lines $57,705 unused while the talent modules decline; BDC is contracted at $4,807 annual with $0 consumed; Catalog sits at 0.5% on $20,071 YTD. The enterprise pattern is 'shelfware at the foundation, thriving at the edges,' which means Stepan is paying for a strategic SAP platform but operating it as a fragmented set of point tools — a posture that will not survive the FY27 renewal cycle when cumulative non-realization becomes visible to procurement leadership.
Convene a single Enterprise Architecture Review in June that treats BTP Integration Suite + BDC + CPEA as the shared substrate for both the Hire-to-Retire and Source-to-Pay process chains, not as three independent BAIP contracts — the agenda must produce one prioritized integration backlog (Recruiting→Onboarding→EC→Payroll, and Sourcing→Catalog→Buying→Expense) with named platform-owner accountability, sequencing CPEA/BDC provisioning in June-July so the foundation is live before the December activation window and before Q3 talent and procurement cycles begin.
Redirect the two large dormant entitlements — HCM Preferred Success ($57,705 unused over 7 months) and Ariba Preferred Success ($9,765/month passive) — into a single Joule-anchored use case workshop within 45 days that forces consumption across the dormant BTP layers (Joule Studio, Integration Suite) while simultaneously unblocking the declining talent and procurement modules; this converts straight-lined CSM spend into the integration and AI authoring work that the architectural diagnosis proves is the actual gap.
Establish a monthly Cross-Domain Consumption & Integration checkpoint (CEA-led, spanning all four L1 areas) with explicit run-rate gates — ~$277/$284 on CPEA lines, ~$1,500/mo on Joule Studio, ~$4,250/mo on Integration Suite, Catalog above 25% by Q3, Concur Expense submitter-base restored — so that platform activation, integration recovery, and application adoption are tracked as one architectural program rather than four separate LoB scorecards, protecting renewal credibility ahead of FY27.

BAIP - Data & AI

YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$3K
YTD Consumed$0
YTD ACV: $0
Full-Year ACV: $0 · Full-Year Budget: $9K
Solution Insights
Stepan's Data & AI architecture is contracted but entirely dormant — SAP Business Data Cloud (the business data fabric meant to anchor mission-critical data products, SAC, and Datasphere) has $0 consumed against its sole December allocation, while the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement that should provide the underlying BDC Public and Database Cloud runtime shows zero usage across all five YTD months against $2,870 of cumulative budget. The LoB has purchased the full Data & AI stack but activated none of it, meaning there is no semantic layer, no data products, and no compute foundation in place to deliver analytics or AI outcomes.
The dependency chain is broken at the foundation — Business Data Cloud cannot deliver its intended capabilities (AI-ready data products, agentic AI grounding, SAC, Datasphere) without an active Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement providing the database and platform runtime. With $4,807 of annual plan across both contracts and only $1,937 + $1,989 + $2,674 of budget still ahead, every month of platform inactivity directly forecloses the December BDC activation window, since BDC onboarding requires 8–12 weeks on a provisioned platform that does not yet exist.
Convene a single joint activation workshop within 30 days that treats CPEA and BDC as one architectural program rather than three separate contracts — sequence CPEA Database Cloud and BDC Public provisioning in June/July so the platform substrate is live before the BDC data product build begins in August, protecting the December consumption target.
Anchor the recovery plan to one end-to-end Data & AI use case (e.g., a Datasphere-connected analytics workload surfaced in SAC) that forces consumption across all three dormant SKUs simultaneously, with monthly checkpoints against the ~$277 and ~$284 run rates needed on the CPEA lines and a hard go-live gate before the December BDC budget month.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
81.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$28K
YTD Consumed$23K
YTD ACV: $0
Full-Year ACV: $0 · Full-Year Budget: $69K
Solution Insights
The Technology Foundation layer at Stepan is architecturally present but functionally stalling — BTP Integration Suite, Joule Studio, and broader Cloud Platform workloads are all trending downward or flat-lining simultaneously, which means the foundation meant to enable extensions, integrations, and AI-driven experiences across every other LoB is contracting rather than scaling. When the integration backbone erodes (Integration Suite down 17% Jan→May) at the same time the AI authoring surface (Joule Studio) never ramps past ~$580/month, the LoB is signaling it has no active pipeline of net-new extension or automation use cases — the platform is being run as a utility for a fixed set of existing flows, not as the innovation substrate it was contracted to be.
Joule Studio's flat $525–$650/month pattern is the most architecturally telling signal — it indicates Stepan has not operationalized Joule as the agentic/AI authoring layer on top of their SAP estate, leaving the Technology Foundation without its intended generative and copilot capabilities. Combined with Integration Suite's decline, this means Stepan is missing the two pillars that make BTP strategic: AI-driven user experiences and the integration fabric that feeds them. With YTD attainment at 51.7% on Joule Studio and 37.2% on Integration Suite against a combined ~$63K annual commitment, roughly half the foundation investment is at risk of non-realization before renewal.
Convene a Technology Foundation architecture review within 30 days with Stepan's platform owner to map active vs. dormant BTP capabilities across Integration Suite, Joule Studio, and Cloud Platform services — the goal is to identify 2–3 net-new use cases (an agentic Joule scenario tied to an existing LoB process, plus one new integration flow) that re-anchor the platform as an innovation layer rather than a static utility, targeting a step-up to ~$1,500/month on Joule Studio and ~$4,250/month on Integration Suite from June onward.
Diagnose the Integration Suite decline before proposing expansion — schedule a technical workload review in the next 2 weeks to determine whether the Jan→May erosion reflects decommissioned interfaces, stalled projects, or workloads migrating to non-SAP middleware, since recovery tactics differ sharply across those root causes; pair this with a monthly consumption checkpoint and milestone reviews in July and September to course-correct before the Q4 renewal-credibility window closes.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
69.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$421K
YTD Consumed$292K
YTD ACV: $94K
Full-Year ACV: $94K · Full-Year Budget: $1.1M
Solution Insights
Stepan's Procure-to-Pay backbone is architecturally fragmented: Ariba Buying and Invoicing is operational but eroding (63.7% avg attainment Mar-May, $67.4K YTD gap), while Ariba Catalog — the compliance enforcement layer that routes buyers to preferred suppliers and negotiated prices — is effectively dormant at 0.5% attainment ($97 of $20,071 YTD). Without an active catalog, Buying and Invoicing has no guided-buying guardrails, which directly explains the maverick spend leakage showing up as declining transactional consumption. Compounding this, Ariba Sourcing sits at 13.9% YTD attainment with May decelerating to $211, meaning the strategic sourcing → contract → catalog → buying chain is broken at three of four links — Stepan is buying without sourcing-derived agreements, without catalog enforcement, and increasingly outside the platform entirely.
The Concur T&E suite is the one architecturally coherent capability in this LoB: Analytics (110% May), ExpenseIt (110.8%), Reporting Services (115%), Conn/Stat/Web/Extract (111.5%), and User Support Desk (111.5%) are all running above plan, indicating receipt capture, data extraction, reporting, and support are working as an integrated workflow. However, two structural cracks threaten this: Concur Expense — the transactional core that feeds every other module — saw ACV collapse 82% in May ($3,684 → $665) with submissions declining for three straight months, and Intelligent Audit is stuck at 64.8% attainment, meaning a shrinking population of expense reports is being processed and only two-thirds of those are being audited for policy compliance. The peripheral analytics and support layers are thriving on top of a contracting and under-governed transactional base.
Re-sequence the Procurement recovery as a connected architecture, not three parallel rescues: start with a Catalog activation workshop in June to enable the top 5 off-platform suppliers (closing the maverick spend leak feeding Ariba Buying's $67.4K gap), then use Preferred Success Ariba's flat $9,765/month entitlement — currently drawn passively — to fund the supplier enablement and sourcing-event acceleration hours needed to lift Ariba Sourcing above 50% attainment by Q3. This converts three dormant/declining contracts and one passive entitlement into a single reinforcing P2P motion.
Diagnose the Concur Expense ACV collapse with Stepan's contract owner within 30 days and run a parallel user-population audit comparing Q1 vs April-May active submitters by business unit — if the May reduction is intentional rightsizing, reset the FY26 plan for Expense and Intelligent Audit (whose $3,073 YTD gap is partly a function of fewer reports to audit); if unintentional, restore the submitter base before the contraction propagates into Analytics, Reporting Services, and ExpenseIt utilization in Q3.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
87.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Budget$220K
YTD Consumed$191K
YTD ACV: $51K
Full-Year ACV: $51K · Full-Year Budget: $533K
Solution Insights
Stepan's HCM foundation is eroding at the core: SF Employee Central — the system of record that feeds payroll, talent, learning, and onboarding — has slid from 95.3% to 91.8% attainment over five months. When the core HRIS is being bypassed for legacy processes, every downstream module inherits the gap, which is exactly what the data shows: Onboarding (41% attainment), Career & Talent Development (51%), and Performance & Goals (76%) are all declining in lockstep. This is not eight independent adoption problems — it is one architectural problem where EC's authoritative employee, org, and position data isn't anchoring the talent suite, so managers and employees revert to offline workflows across the lifecycle.
The Hire-to-Retire process chain is contracted but operationally fragmented: SF Recruiting is flat-lined at ~$3,938/month (single static usage pattern), SF Onboarding is declining to $348/month (41% attainment), and EC Payroll is locked in a $4,759–$4,906 band indicating a fixed employee subset is being processed while in-scope populations run outside the system. The architecture is designed for Recruiting → Onboarding → EC → Payroll to operate as one continuous flow, but the consumption signatures show three disconnected islands — candidates are being hired without flowing into Onboarding, new hires aren't landing in EC, and EC employees aren't all reaching Payroll. Meanwhile $57,705 of remaining Preferred Success entitlement is straight-lining unused, forfeiting the very CSM and expert capacity that should be unblocking these integration gaps.
Convene a single Hire-to-Retire architecture review with Stepan's HR/HRIS lead in June that treats Recruiting, Onboarding, EC, and EC Payroll as one process chain rather than four products — specifically audit how many Jan–May hires flowed end-to-end through all four systems versus dropped into manual handoffs, and identify which legal entities or employee populations are contracted for EC Payroll but still processed externally. This addresses the flat-line signatures in Recruiting and Payroll and the decline in Onboarding with one diagnosis.
Redirect the unused Preferred Success entitlement ($57,705 over 7 months) into a calendared talent-cycle activation plan that anchors SF Performance & Goals, Compensation, and Career & Talent Development to Stepan's FY2026 HR operating rhythm (mid-year reviews, merit planning, development planning windows). Book specific expert sessions and adoption clinics against each cycle milestone now — this converts straight-lined CSM spend into the recurring process cadence that the declining talent-module consumption proves is currently missing.

Budget vs Consumed by Customer
Stepan Company
YTD ACV: $145K
YTD Budget: $672K
YTD Consumed: $506K
Full-Year ACV: $145K
Full-Year Budget: $1.7M
Industry Insights
Stepan Company is the sole customer in this Chemicals peer group, which limits direct peer comparison but reveals an industry-critical architectural failure: the integration and data foundation (BTP Integration Suite down 17%, Business Data Cloud at $0, CPEA at $0) that specialty chemicals companies require for connected operations is completely dormant. In an industry where procurement spend optimization, regulatory compliance, and workforce safety are tightly coupled, Stepan's fragmented point-tool architecture — with Hire-to-Retire and Source-to-Pay chains operating as disconnected islands — prevents the integrated chemical manufacturing operating model that justifies enterprise SAP investment and exposes the company to compliance and cost-control gaps that competitors with active integration fabrics would not face.
Stepan exhibits a 'shelfware at the foundation, thriving at the edges' pattern that is particularly damaging for chemicals companies: analytics and reporting modules (Concur analytics >110%) run strong while transactional cores collapse (Concur Expense 82% May decline), and strategic platform components (BDC $4,807 contracted/$0 consumed, Catalog at 0.5%) sit idle. For a specialty chemicals manufacturer where raw material price volatility and supplier risk require tight Source-to-Pay integration, this inverted consumption profile means Stepan can report on spend but cannot control it — a competitive disadvantage against chemicals peers who have activated their procurement platforms end-to-end.
Stepan's dormant Preferred Success entitlements ($57,705 HCM unused, $9,765/month Ariba passive) represent the largest immediate consumption recovery opportunity in this portfolio. In the chemicals industry where EHS-trained workforce availability and supplier qualification directly impact plant uptime and regulatory standing, these unused advisory services could be redirected toward activating the integration and AI layers (Joule Studio flat at $580/month) that would connect talent pipelines to operational readiness and procurement workflows to compliance — capabilities that leading chemicals companies treat as operational necessities, not optional enhancements.
The complete absence of AI and advanced analytics foundation activation (Joule Studio flat, Business Data Cloud $0, CPEA $0) places Stepan behind the trajectory that chemicals industry leaders are pursuing. As the sector moves toward predictive demand planning, automated regulatory reporting, and AI-assisted formulation management, Stepan's dormant AI substrate means the company cannot participate in these capability shifts regardless of application-layer adoption — a strategic gap that will widen with each quarter of platform inactivity.
Convene a Chemicals Operating Model Architecture Review in June positioning BTP Integration Suite, Business Data Cloud, and CPEA as the shared substrate for specialty chemicals operations — not as three separate technology contracts — with explicit focus on connecting Hire-to-Retire (workforce safety, EHS certification tracking) and Source-to-Pay (raw material procurement, supplier compliance) into the integrated chemical manufacturing process chains that drive regulatory compliance and cost control; this single intervention addresses the root cause behind all four L1 domain consumption gaps.
Redirect the $67,470+ in dormant Preferred Success entitlements (HCM and Ariba combined) into a 45-day Joule-anchored integration workshop that forces activation across the dormant platform layers while simultaneously addressing the declining talent and procurement modules; for a chemicals company, prioritize use cases around supplier risk scoring, EHS workforce certification workflows, and raw material spend analytics — capabilities that translate platform activation into measurable operational outcomes.
Establish a monthly Cross-Domain Platform Health checkpoint with explicit chemicals-industry run-rate gates: CPEA activation by July for regulatory analytics, Integration Suite restored to $4,250+/month to support batch traceability and supplier connectivity, Catalog above 25% by Q3 to enable raw material standardization, and Concur Expense submitter-base restored to ensure T&E compliance across plant operations — tracking these as one architectural program rather than four LoB scorecards to protect the $144,897 ACV renewal credibility ahead of FY27.

S.C. Johnson & Son, Inc.
YTD ACV: $214K
YTD Budget: $735K

Full-Year ACV: $214K
Full-Year Budget: $1.8M
Industry Insights
Kraft Heinz leads this Consumer Products cohort on transactional execution breadth — Ariba Buying at 111%, Business Network transmitting POs, PaPM bursting to ceiling, and Joule Studio scaling — but is simultaneously the most architecturally fragile because its integration fabric (Integration Suite eroding from 134% to 80% attainment, BTP Database Cloud at 0%, SAC BI at $0 consumed) is collapsing beneath these workloads. Domino's and S.C. Johnson show healthier integration plumbing (Integration Suite at 99% and declining but still functional respectively), yet neither has activated the intelligence layers above. The net effect: Kraft Heinz is running the most SAP workloads but on the weakest foundation, creating the highest near-term disruption risk in the peer group.
S.C. Johnson exhibits the most severe 'inverted pyramid' architecture in this cohort — value layers like BDC analytics (4-5x ceiling) and Concur ExpenseIt are over-consuming while the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement foundation sits at effectively 0% across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance. Neither Domino's (HANA Cloud at 75%, SAP Build at 53%) nor Kraft Heinz (BTP Public at 1326% but Database Cloud at 0%) shows this complete foundation void. For a CPG company dependent on rapid reformulation and supplier resiliency, S.C. Johnson's missing BTP runtime means every cross-domain integration — PLM-to-manufacturing, Concur-to-Blackline-to-S/4, BDC persistence — runs on manual workarounds that will fail at scale or during the next S/4 upgrade cycle.
All three Consumer Products customers share an identical architectural anti-pattern: transactional/execution layers are live and scaling while governance, intelligence, and supplier collaboration layers are dormant or declining — Domino's has Ariba Buying at 99.96% but Sourcing/Contracts at 66% and Business Network flat; Kraft Heinz runs Ariba Buying at 111% but Sourcing at 35%, Spend Analysis dark, and Supplier Risk at $0; S.C. Johnson has Business Network frozen at $48.6K monthly with IPD at 5.7%. For an industry facing commodity inflation, ESG traceability mandates, and private-label competition, this pattern means none of these enterprises can execute the spend governance, supplier risk visibility, or forecast collaboration that defends CPG margin — they are paying for connected value chains but operating as disconnected transaction processors.
Domino's is the only customer in this cohort with a contractually coherent BTP-anchored clean-core strategy (Build + Integration Suite + Custom Domain + HANA Cloud), but execution has stalled — SAP Build collapsed to 52.7%, Custom Domain is flat, and no net-new franchisee or loyalty apps have launched. Kraft Heinz and S.C. Johnson never contracted this extensibility vision at comparable depth. This positions Domino's as the peer group's best candidate for rapid BTP activation because the commercial and architectural foundation exists; the gap is purely operational activation, whereas Kraft Heinz and S.C. Johnson would require contract restructuring before similar workloads could land.
Launch a coordinated 'Governance Layer Activation' initiative across all three customers within 60 days, targeting the shared dormant intelligence and control products: Ariba Sourcing/Contracts and Supplier Risk reactivation on 3-5 anchor spend categories at each company, Business Network forecast collaboration tied to IBP or demand planning cycles, and Spend Analysis dashboards sourced from existing Buying transaction data. Frame this as a CPG margin-defense program — supplier risk visibility, sourcing compliance, and demand-supply synchronization are table-stakes capabilities that all three have contracted but none has operationalized, and the VAT can run a single enablement motion with industry-specific use cases rather than three isolated product conversations.
Prioritize Kraft Heinz for an emergency Integration Suite and BTP foundation recovery sprint before end of Q2 — the combination of eroding integration fabric and accelerating consumption layers creates the highest near-term disruption risk in the portfolio. Convene a joint architecture session with their CIO and Chief Supply Chain Officer within 30 days to mandate Integration Suite as the backbone for all Joule, Ariba, Business Network, and Signavio flows, and activate BTP Database Cloud and SAC BI on one anchor use case (finance dashboard or supply chain KPI layer) to arrest the regression before the $3.5M+ Business Network investment and scaling AI agents become stranded on point-to-point plumbing.
Use Domino's as the BTP activation proof point for the cohort — stand up a 90-day 'BTP Activation Squad' to land 3-5 net-new workloads (franchisee portal, supplier onboarding handoff, store-level demand sensing) that consume the already-contracted HANA Cloud, SAP Build, Integration Suite, and Custom Domain stack end-to-end. Success at Domino's creates a replicable CPG playbook the VAT can adapt for Kraft Heinz's Joule and Signavio extensibility needs and S.C. Johnson's eventual CPEA activation, converting one customer's stalled clean-core ambition into an industry-wide consumption catalyst.
Initiate a unified FY26 contract rebaselining conversation across the cohort before Q3 close, treating over-consumption and under-consumption as a single commercial event at each customer: true-up Domino's Concur T&E (breach risk by October) and Kraft Heinz's PaPM/BTP Public (already at ceiling) while converting dormant ACV — an estimated $200K+ at Domino's, $2M+ at Kraft Heinz, and $500K+ at S.C. Johnson — into contractually-linked activation commitments tied to the governance and foundation roadmaps above. This repositions FY27 renewals from fragmented downsizing negotiations into enterprise renewal conversations anchored to demonstrated business value.
Life sciences and healthcare

Budget vs Consumed by Customer
Abbott Laboratories
YTD ACV: $436K
YTD Budget: $1.8M
YTD Consumed: $1.5M
Full-Year ACV: $436K
Full-Year Budget: $4.3M
Eli Lilly & Company
YTD ACV: $1.8M
YTD Budget: $7.6M
YTD Consumed: $7.3M
Full-Year ACV: $1.8M
Full-Year Budget: $18.8M
Industry Insights
Abbott Laboratories leads Eli Lilly on integration infrastructure maturity — Abbott's Integration Suite runs at 107-112% of plan with active middleware flows, while Lilly's Integration Suite is flatlined at ~$25K/month with $24.6K YTD unused and BDC Public collapsed 79%. However, this leadership is hollow: Abbott's integration output terminates nowhere downstream, meaning both companies share the same architectural failure mode — integration fabric disconnected from business process closure — but Abbott has at least built the pipes while Lilly has not yet turned them on.
Eli Lilly is ahead of Abbott on AI and process intelligence adoption — Joule Studio at 171% and Signavio PM expanded 2.5x demonstrate Lilly is actively investing in the agent and process mining surfaces that define next-generation pharma operations, while Abbott shows no comparable AI or process intelligence consumption signals. For life sciences companies facing pressure to accelerate drug development cycles and manufacturing efficiency, Lilly's bet on process intelligence positions them ahead of Abbott on operational transformation — but without the integration substrate to connect these investments, Lilly risks building intelligent islands rather than an intelligent enterprise.
Both Abbott and Eli Lilly exhibit an identical and industry-critical architectural anti-pattern: fully consumed enablement and success wrappers sitting atop dormant platform workloads. Abbott over-consumes Integration Suite, SIM, and Preferred Success Ariba while SAC, HANA Cloud, Build, and Ariba Buying lag; Lilly pins all Preferred Success entitlements at 100%+ while HXM Operations (38%), Commerce Cloud (45%), SAC Planning (4.5%), and DRC (0.17%) remain shelfware. In life sciences, where regulatory scrutiny demands demonstrable value from technology investments, both companies are paying premium prices for guided adoption that is not converting into production GxP workloads — exposing a combined ~$700K+ of ACV to renewal risk across the cohort.
Eli Lilly is significantly behind Abbott on pharma-critical GxP and regulated execution readiness — Lilly's Batch Release Hub sits at $0 consumption against $32K/month ACV, Document & Reporting Compliance is at 0.17% across 90+ countries, and IBP Supply/Inventory/Response modules are dormant, leaving the digital thread from demand signal through batch release to statutory reporting broken at three of four nodes. Abbott's supply chain anchor (~$1.48M annual) and IBP investment at least show ratable consumption, even if under-activated. For pharmaceutical manufacturers where electronic batch disposition and e-invoicing/VAT controls are audit-critical, Lilly's dormant GxP stack represents a material compliance and commercial exposure that Abbott has partially mitigated.
Execute a coordinated 'Closed-Loop Architecture Review' across both Abbott and Eli Lilly within Q2 FY2026 — position this as an industry-standard intervention for life sciences companies where regulatory and operational integrity depends on end-to-end process closure (source-to-pay, plan-to-deliver, demand-to-batch-release, record-to-report). Use Abbott's over-consumed Integration Suite as the positive proof point for Lilly's dormant integration fabric, and use Lilly's Joule/Signavio investments as the aspirational target for Abbott's next-phase AI activation — creating peer pressure and cross-customer learning that accelerates consumption at both accounts.
Launch an industry-specific 'Preferred Success Re-Pointing' initiative across the cohort within 60 days that contractually redirects all fully-consumed success entitlements at both Abbott and Lilly to named, under-adopted platform workloads aligned to GxP and regulated-execution priorities — specifically targeting Batch Release Hub, DRC statutory reporting, IBP module activation, and SAC Planning go-live. Frame this to both CIOs as an industry-standard practice for pharma companies to ensure success spend produces audit-defensible platform adoption rather than generic advisory ahead of FY27 renewals.
Escalate a joint VAT briefing to SAP industry leadership on the systemic 'enablers over outcomes' pattern observed across both Abbott and Lilly — the cohort has contracted for $2.2M ACV but is burning integration, success, and incentive capacity while leaving planning, procurement execution, AI substrate, and GxP automation dormant. Recommend a life sciences practice playbook that sequences platform adoption to match regulatory and operational value chains, preventing future customers from replicating this architectural debt and giving the VAT a repeatable framework to protect the $9.4M budget across the cohort.

Budget vs Consumed by Customer
Marathon Petroleum Company LP
YTD ACV: $222K
YTD Budget: $805K
YTD Consumed: $767K
Full-Year ACV: $222K
Full-Year Budget: $2.0M
Industry Insights
Marathon Petroleum is operating as a fragmented SAP environment rather than an integrated digital core — the refining sector's margin optimization imperative demands real-time signal integration across spend, operations, and financial data, yet Marathon's BTP integration backbone (BDC Public at 0%, Integration Suite dormant, Finance BTP at zero consumption) leaves S/4HANA, Ariba, Signavio, SAC Planning, and Joule functioning as five disconnected product islands, directly blocking the closed-loop visibility that differentiates high-performing downstream operators from laggards in volatile crack-spread environments.
Marathon exhibits a systemic 'execution without governance' anti-pattern that creates material risk for refining operations — Database Cloud at 537% consumption versus BDC at 0%, Joule Studio at 320% versus dormant Integration Suite, and Ariba Buying at 138% versus Catalog at 35% means the company is industrializing transactional throughput while starving the control layers that enforce compliance, spend discipline, and architectural coherence, a posture that in the highly regulated downstream energy sector accelerates off-contract spend, audit exposure, and margin leakage simultaneously.
Marathon's commercial envelope is materially misaligned with actual consumption architecture — projected overages of ~$108K on Database Cloud, ~$155K on Ariba Buying, and 3.2x plan on Joule Studio will collide with 35-100% stranded entitlements on BDC Public, BTP Foundation, Finance BTP, Ariba Catalog, and Preferred Success, meaning Marathon will exit FY2026 having overpaid for runtime while underutilizing the data product, integration, and advisory capabilities that convert SAP investments into the margin discipline and operational agility refining economics demand.
As a single-customer cohort, Marathon Petroleum represents both the capability leader and the laggard within this oil and gas portfolio — leading on transactional platform adoption (S/4HANA Private Cloud, Ariba P2P, SAC Planning, Joule all actively consuming) but lagging critically on the integration and data fabric layer that downstream energy operators require to correlate crude acquisition costs, refining throughput, and product margin in real time; without peer benchmarks in this portfolio, Marathon's own contracted-but-dormant BTP/BDC architecture serves as the internal baseline it is failing to meet.
Within 30 days, convene a Chief Architect-level Enterprise Integration and Data Fabric review with Marathon that treats BTP, Integration Suite, and BDC as one unified backbone for refining margin optimization — map each over-consuming workload (Joule agents for operational intelligence, Ariba Buying for feedstock procurement, Database Cloud for transactional scale, Signavio findings for process efficiency, SAC Planning models for margin forecasting) to the specific APIs, events, and governed data products each requires, and commit a sequenced June-September activation plan with named business owners tied to Marathon's H2 planning cycle.
Execute a consolidated FY2026 commercial true-up and FY2027 baseline reset within 45 days that addresses Database Cloud, Ariba Buying, and Joule Studio overages as a single package, funded by repurposing structurally underutilized entitlements (BDC Public, BTP Foundation Other, Finance BTP, Ariba Catalog headroom, Preferred Success) — this prevents mid-year service constraints while re-anchoring contract shape to Marathon's true consumption architecture and strengthening the renewal narrative around business outcomes rather than infrastructure overage management.
Launch a 'governance and control layer' rescue program within 14 days as a single CFO/COO-sponsored executive conversation covering three starved control points — Ariba Catalog re-enablement to enforce spend compliance on feedstock and MRO procurement, Preferred Success sponsor re-alignment to provide S/4HANA Private Cloud architectural guidance, and Signavio Process Management CoE cadence linked to Process Intelligence findings for continuous refining process optimization — framing the intervention as margin discipline infrastructure essential to downstream energy competitiveness rather than isolated technology gaps.

Budget vs Consumed by Customer
Border States Industries, Inc.
YTD ACV: $82K
YTD Budget: $454K
YTD Consumed: $410K
Full-Year ACV: $82K
Full-Year Budget: $1.1M
Industry Insights
Border States Industries is the sole representative in this wholesale distribution cohort, making it the de facto benchmark — but measured against industry-standard architectural maturity, the company is operating as five disconnected point solutions rather than one integrated wholesale distribution platform, which is a critical deficiency given that wholesale distributors live and die by inventory velocity, margin protection, and customer service speed that only cross-domain data flows can enable.
Border States shows an inverted consumption pattern that is architecturally unhealthy for wholesale distribution: the Technology Foundation layer (BTP, Integration Suite, Joule) is running hot at 119-209% while the business outcome layers — Data & AI at 62% unallocated, Customer Experience with a $183K ACV gap, HCM Compensation at 0% — remain cold. In wholesale distribution, where same-day fulfillment and dynamic pricing require real-time data orchestration, burning platform capacity without wiring it to Commerce, Finance, or Analytics means Border States is paying for integration horsepower that never reaches the warehouse floor or the customer portal.
The absence of a BDC-to-Datasphere/SAC data pipeline is a wholesale distribution-specific risk for Border States — distributors competing against Amazon Business and Grainger require demand sensing, inventory optimization, and margin analytics that only a functioning data fabric can deliver. Border States has the Business Data Cloud infrastructure but no consumption surface, leaving pricing decisions, inventory positioning, and customer segmentation as manual or legacy-system-dependent processes while $215K of Data & AI budget sits dormant.
Border States' Commerce Cloud implementation without Service, Marketing, or CDP integration creates a broken customer experience loop that wholesale distribution competitors have already closed — B2B buyers expect account-specific pricing, order history visibility, and proactive service alerts as table stakes. Running Commerce as a standalone storefront rather than an orchestrated customer engagement platform puts Border States at a competitive disadvantage against digitally mature distributors who treat every customer touchpoint as a data capture and service opportunity.
Execute an Enterprise Architecture Review in Q3 with Border States' executive leadership (CIO, CFO, CHRO, Head of Digital Commerce) to map the four critical integration gaps — Performance-to-Compensation, BDC-to-Datasphere/SAC, Commerce-to-Service/Marketing, and Concur Expense-to-Pay/Audit — onto a single Integration Suite roadmap that converts existing BTP overconsumption into the connective tissue wholesale distribution operations require for inventory visibility, margin optimization, and unified customer experience.
Initiate a FY2026 contract rebalancing conversation within 45 days that ties the required Technology Foundation and Concur true-ups (~$17K+) to explicit activation commitments on dormant ACV — SuccessFactors Compensation for the next merit cycle, one net-new Commerce Cloud workload integrating Service or Marketing, and one Data & AI use case on BDC for demand sensing or margin analytics — ensuring commercial expansion funds outcome delivery rather than point-solution accumulation.
Launch a 90-day wholesale distribution-specific use case sprint focused on three high-impact integration flows: (1) real-time inventory and pricing data from BDC into Datasphere for demand-responsive stocking, (2) Commerce Cloud to Service Cloud integration for order-to-issue resolution visibility, and (3) unified spend analytics connecting Concur to SAC for procurement cost optimization — each flow directly addresses margin protection and customer retention priorities that define wholesale distribution competitiveness.
